PRD: Shadow Traffic for Quality Eval Metrics (Console & Rollouts)

## Summary
Teams trust Redwood for safe rollouts based on latency/cost/error SLOs, but still lack a production-safe way to detect *quality* regressions (correctness, safety, instruction following, format adherence) when changing models/versions/quantization/prompt templates. This PRD proposes **Shadow Traffic**: duplicate a sampled subset of live requests to one or more candidate variants, score outputs asynchronously, and surface **quality deltas** alongside existing performance and error metrics—without affecting the user-visible response.

Shadow Traffic is designed to work across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments using existing routing/rollout primitives (route configs, canaries, A/B) and Redwood’s eval-harness scoring templates.

Source event id: activity-known-issue-streaming-responses-may-freeze-db2f03cb51e6

Actor: elliot-price
Occurred at: 1771070400000
Content:
Known issue: Streaming responses may freeze mid-generation (SSE stalls)

## Overview
Some customers have reported that streaming responses stop emitting tokens mid-generation while the HTTP connection remains open. The client may not receive an error or a terminal [DONE] event. This is typically described as a *freeze* or *stall* in server-sent events (SSE).

## Symptoms
- Streaming completion stops mid-sentence.
- TCP/TLS connection remains open.
- No final event (e.g., [DONE]) is observed by the client.
- Client UI shows an infinite spinner or hangs waiting for more tokens.

## Where weve seen it
- Dedicated and Hosted API traffic, primarily under higher concurrency or with intermediaries (proxies/LBs).
- Internal reproduction was easiest when response compression (gzip) was applied to text/event-stream.

## Primary contributing factors (current understanding)
1. Buffering/backpressure in intermediaries when payload chunks are small (token-by-token).
2. ...

Actor: evan-liu

Content:
Interconnect saturation sweep - empirical results and notes

Purpose:
Quick empirical sweep to understand where interconnect limits show up for our inference serving runtime when moving from single-node NVLink-bound configs to cross-node InfiniBand (IB) + GPUDirect flows. Goal: produce actionable mitigations for production Dedicated racks (A100/H100 mixes) and guidance for Private deployments.

Summary / headline findings (short):
- Same-node NVLink moves huge bulk of heavy-model KV exchange; saturation visible on large tensor-parallel allreduce and KV checkpoint broadcast at high batch/seq combos.
- Cross-node (IB HDR/200) with GPUDirect/RDMA gives decent bandwidth but higher latency and markedly larger CPU involvement for small/irregular message shapes; NCCL microbenchmarks optimistic vs real app patterns.
- Pipeline-parallel schedules that create many medium-sized collectives suffer the most when crossing nodes; tensor-parallel ring allreduc...

Content:
burst-windowed-batcher-inspection-notes

Summary:

This is a working note for the burst-windowed batcher investigation — a lightweight scheduler variant we prototyped to handle brief, high-intensity microbursts without inflating average latency for stable traffic. The goal: reduce 99.9/99.99 tail spikes during 5--30s bursts while preserving throughput and keeping token cost per request close to baseline.

Motivation / Background:
- Continuous batching gives great throughput, but we still see tail spikes when short bursts of traffic arrive and the normal coalescer forms large, heterogeneous batches that block low-latency requests.
- Prior experiments (see RED-2718) looked at timeout annealing and urgency weights; this note explores a windowed-batcher that adaptively creates short-duration "burst windows" with aggressive small-batch preference and fast flush policy.

Context: We see frequent oscillation in short-lived A/B experiments when decisions are driven by high-variance per-cohort metrics (latency/quality) causing noisy promotes/rollbacks and churn in serving capacity. This PR implements a lightweight "cumulative signal pinning" system: cohorts are evaluated against a time-weighted, cumulative metric fingerprint rather than instant window snapshots. Content:
Tenant failover orchestrator: backpressure annealing patch and graceful cold-starts

Context and motivation: During last week's regional failover drill we observed repeated rapid open/close cycles of tenant-level circuit breakers when background traffic spiked while instances were cold. This caused latency spikes and increased error-profile churn for top-tier tenants. Root cause: a hard threshold for open/close plus aggressive immediate retry semantics combined with KV-cache reattachment delays. This PR introduces an annealing-based backpressure controller plus a short soft-warmup window to reduce oscillation and give KV/worker pools time to warm.

Source app: github
Source URL: /github?repo=perf-canary&tab=pulls&pr=175888
Actor: aisha-rahman
Occurred at: 1768910400000
Content:
Add holistic cohorting and time-warp probing to improve regression sensitivity

Summary: This PR adds two coordinated features to the perf-canary stack: (1) a holistic cohorting service that groups traffic by behavioral fingerprints (payload size, sequence length, model variant, and feature flags) so canary probes exercise representative cohorts, and (2) a time-warp prober that synthesizes historical traffic slices at shifted time horizons (day-of-week / hour-of-day offsets) to surface regressions that only appear under certain temporal contexts. Motivation: We observed recurring regressions that escaped standard canary windows because they were conditioned on traffic cohorts or only appeared at specific daily patterns. By combining cohorted sampling with horizon-shifted probes, we increase sensitivity to slow, covariate-dependent degradations without increasing probe volume or false positives.

Content:
Q1 2026 Perf Baseline Plan (Serving Runtime)

## Purpose
This page defines the **Q1 2026 “golden” performance baselines** for Redwood’s serving runtime and the plan to produce them in a reproducible, CI-backed way.

The baseline is used for:
- Runtime release signoff (release branches)
- Perf-canary regression detection (nightly + gated modes)
- Model catalog performance profiles (internal first; optional downstream publication)

## Goals (Q1 2026)
1) Publish baseline **latency/throughput** numbers for top deployed models across priority hardware and regions.
2) Establish a **representative workload taxonomy** that mirrors production traffic shapes.
3) Ensure results are **reproducible and attributable** (strict env capture, fixed workload seeds, manifests, trace linkage).
4) Integrate into automated canaries and release gating with clear thresholds for **regression vs noise**.

## Non-goals
- New model onboarding beyond what’s r...

Summary

This playbook defines the evidence orchestration, collection, and packaging process for incidents that originate from model-serving failures in multi-tenant environments. It is focused on preserving chain-of-custody, meeting cross-border data residency constraints, and producing artifacts suitable for internal investigations and external audits.

Scope

- Applies to incidents where a model-serving event affects more than one tenant or where tenant isolation may be implicated (data leakage, cross-tenant inference responses, or authorization bypass).
- Covers services: inference-gateway, model-router, kv-prefix-cache, tokenizer-service, audit-collector, and inference-observability.
- Excludes: single-tenant application errors unrelated to model outputs (those follow standard app incident process).

Content:
Cumulative signal pinning for more stable A/B experiments and timed rollback hedging

Context: We see frequent oscillation in short-lived A/B experiments when decisions are driven by high-variance per-cohort metrics (latency/quality) causing noisy promotes/rollbacks and churn in serving capacity. This PR implements a lightweight "cumulative signal pinning" system: cohorts are evaluated against a time-weighted, cumulative metric fingerprint rather than instant window snapshots. The evaluation path produces a pinned cohort decision (promote/hold/rollback) that is stable for a configurable grace period and backed by a compact snapshot used for deterministic rollback and replay. Motivation: Reduce noisy automated rollbacks and reduce thrash on Dedicated pools; preserve fast reaction for true regressions while making borderline signals less likely to flip-flop. Actor: ava-chen
Occurred at: 1768910400000
Content:
OpenAI toolcall consensus resolver and affordance mapping

Context and motivation: We observed a class of customer bugs where OpenAI-compatible tool calls produced multiple candidate tool invocations or partially overlapping schema outputs in streaming scenarios (e.g., assistant suggests both 'create_ticket' and 'open_case' with overlapping fields). Existing heuristics (first-wins / highest-confidence) are brittle when tools have similar signatures or when streaming fragments arrive out-of-order. This PR implements a lightweight consensus resolver and an affordance mapping layer that: 1) aggregates partial and final tool outputs during a request, 2) scores candidates by schema affinity, declared affordances, and runtime provenance, and 3) emits a single canonical invoke envelope to downstream backends while preserving a decision provenance header for audit and debugging.

Context and motivation: customer incidents and internal SLO runs showed repeated small latency drifts during streaming responses that were not captured by existing per-request percentiles. These drifts correlate with bursty token emission patterns (short, frequent microbursts) and cross-tenant billing anomalies. This PR introduces two complementary observability primitives: (1) a stream-cadence fingerprint metric that summarizes emission cadence per invocation, and (2) a cost-mirroring pipeline that writes compact per-invocation cost samples to the billing index for reconciliation and anomaly detection.

Source event id: activity-389007-94d189276a8f
Source app: github
Source URL: /github?repo=redwood&tab=pulls&pr=389007
Actor: ava-chen
Occurred at: 1768910400000
Content:
Tenant failover orchestrator: backpressure annealing patch and graceful cold-starts

Context and motivation: During last week's regional failover drill we observed repeated rapid open/close cycles of tenant-level circuit breakers when background traffic spiked while instances were cold. This caused latency spikes and increased error-profile churn for top-tier tenants. Root cause: a hard threshold for open/close plus aggressive immediate retry semantics combined with KV-cache reattachment delays. This PR introduces an annealing-based backpressure controller plus a short soft-warmup window to reduce oscillation and give KV/worker pools time to warm.

Source app: github
Source URL: /github?repo=redwood-private-installer&tab=pulls&pr=24979
Actor: ethan-park
Occurred at: 1768910400000
Content:
Add break-glass flag for bundle verification bypass + explicit audit warning

Context: We now fail-closed on offline bundle signature/manifest verification during preflight. For a small set of air-gapped environments, we still need an emergency, explicitly-audited path to proceed when verification cannot be performed (e.g., corrupted trust store update package, temporary time source issues, or operational incident). This PR introduces a break-glass override flag that is policy-gated and leaves strong evidence (console warnings + local audit markers) when used.

What this PR does: Adds a break-glass path for preflight/install that allows skipping bundle signature verification only when an explicit local policy allows it. The override is intentionally noisy and requires operators to provide a reason + approval reference, and we emit audit events for both attempted and successful usage.

CLI / UX changes: - New flag: --break-glass-skip-bundle-verification (alias: ...

Source event id: activity-175888-30817df76cef
Source app: github
Source URL: /github?repo=perf-canary&tab=pulls&pr=175888
Actor: aisha-rahman
Occurred at: 1768910400000
Content:
Add holistic cohorting and time-warp probing to improve regression sensitivity

Summary: This PR adds two coordinated features to the perf-canary stack: (1) a holistic cohorting service that groups traffic by behavioral fingerprints (payload size, sequence length, model variant, and feature flags) so canary probes exercise representative cohorts, and (2) a time-warp prober that synthesizes historical traffic slices at shifted time horizons (day-of-week / hour-of-day offsets) to surface regressions that only appear under certain temporal contexts. Motivation: We observed recurring regressions that escaped standard canary windows because they were conditioned on traffic cohorts or only appeared at specific daily patterns. By combining cohorted sampling with horizon-shifted probes, we increase sensitivity to slow, covariate-dependent degradations without increasing probe volume or false positives.

---

Actor: ethan-park
Occurred at: 1771675200000
Content:
Add RBAC permissions for rollout auto-rollback suppression/override (admin-only)

Requester: Felix Schneider (Console views / auditability)

Context
As part of the Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration, we are adding:
- Admin-only suppression windows ("do not auto-rollback for X")
- Admin-only manual override actions (pause/rollback) when auto-actions are enabled

We need RBAC updates so these controls are not available to general Console admins or service owners by default, and all usage is auditable.

Current behavior
- In staging, suppression/override endpoints are behind a feature flag but currently rely on a coarse "console_admin" check.
- This is too broad for production; Security requested explicit permissions and audit trail requirements before enabling for production services.

Content:
Adaptive commit phasing and ephemeral slots PRD (WIP)

Purpose:
This doc sketches a new approach for Dedicated capacity that smooths customer commit shocks and enables short-lived ephemeral capacity to absorb spikes with predictable isolation and cost behavior. Idea is to separate long-lived committed slots from short-lived ephemeral slots and introduce a commit-phasing controller plus a stochastic smoothing ledger so we can guarantee tenant isolation while giving flexible burst capacity.

Context / problem:
- Customers want the predictability of reserved capacity but also need cheap transient headroom for unpredictable spikes (batch prep, promotions, flash usage).
- Existing designs either over-provision (expensive) or allow noisy neighbors during bursts.
- Need better SLO-aligned degradation: preserve critical low-latency lanes while allowing best-effort workloads to be time-shifted or throttled.

Content:
Hydrated session protocol and SDK concessions (PRD - WIP)

TL;DR: Propose a "Hydrated Session" protocol that makes hosted streaming + tool-calls robust for intermittent clients (mobile, edge) by allowing compact session snapshots, incremental hydration of state, and an ergonomic SDK surface that accepts partial responses and resolves to typed outputs. Goals: 1) Keep tail-latency small for stable clients while enabling reconnect/resume for flaky networks. 2) Support typed tool-call handshakes and deterministic replay of tool results when possible. 3) Provide easy-to-adopt SDK primitives that minimize client code and reduce server-side complexity for common patterns.

Actor: asha-patel
Occurred at: 1741089600000
Content:
KV shadowing and op-offload exploration

Summary

This note captures a set of small, targeted experiments we ran to understand the tradeoffs of shadowing KV-cache state while opportunistically offloading compute-heavy ops between Triton, TensorRT (TRT), and our custom CUDA kernels. The goal: identify low-effort runtime heuristics that reduce tail latency regressions when we switch kernels or fallback between runtimes, while preserving burst throughput and KV cache hit quality.

Why this matters (short):
- Model serving latency is sensitive to small changes in where attention/softmax/gelu kernels run.
- A kernel switch can invalidate assumptions about KV-format, alignment, and hidden-dtype, causing micro-regressions that cascade into tail spikes.
- We want graceful fallbacks with minimal artifacts (no model re-serialize) and minimal cache churn.

Key ideas explored:
1) KV-shadowing: maintain a parallel, lightweight shadow cac...

Content:
Poisson-burst adaptive warmup: throughput harness design notes

Purpose and scope:

This note documents an alternative throughput harness approach we experimented with: a Poisson/Burst interarrival load generator combined with an adaptive warmup/priming phase. Goal is to get stable, repeatable throughput numbers while exercising realistic burstiness patterns and ensuring internal caches (KV, kernel caches, cuBLAS heuristics) and model memory paths are representative of production. This is not the final canonical protocol but captures observations, configuration recommendations, and acceptance criteria for repeatable bench runs.

Why a Poisson + burst model?
- Real traffic is rarely perfectly fixed-interval or completely uniform. Poisson arrivals more closely approximate user-driven API traffic for many product use cases, and explicit short bursts model microburst behavior from multiplexed frontends or sudden fan-out.

This doc sketches a new approach for Dedicated capacity that smooths customer commit shocks and enables short-lived ephemeral capacity to absorb spikes with predictable isolation and cost behavior. Hydrated session protocol and SDK concessions (PRD - WIP)

TL;DR: Propose a "Hydrated Session" protocol that makes hosted streaming + tool-calls robust for intermittent clients (mobile, edge) by allowing compact session snapshots, incremental hydration of state, and an ergonomic SDK surface that accepts partial responses and resolves to typed outputs. Goals: 1) Keep tail-latency small for stable clients while enabling reconnect/resume for flaky networks. 2) Support typed tool-call handshakes and deterministic replay of tool results when possible. 3) Provide easy-to-adopt SDK primitives that minimize client code and reduce server-side complexity for common patterns.

Actor: asha-patel

Content:
KV shadowing and op-offload exploration

Summary

This note captures a set of small, targeted experiments we ran to understand the tradeoffs of shadowing KV-cache state while opportunistically offloading compute-heavy ops between Triton, TensorRT (TRT), and our custom CUDA kernels. The goal: identify low-effort runtime heuristics that reduce tail latency regressions when we switch kernels or fallback between runtimes, while preserving burst throughput and KV cache hit quality.

Why this matters (short):
- Model serving latency is sensitive to small changes in where attention/softmax/gelu kernels run.
- A kernel switch can invalidate assumptions about KV-format, alignment, and hidden-dtype, causing micro-regressions that cascade into tail spikes.
- We want graceful fallbacks with minimal artifacts (no model re-serialize) and minimal cache churn.

Key ideas explored:
1) KV-shadowing: maintain a parallel, lightweight shadow cac...

Actor: ava-chen

Content:
Poisson-burst adaptive warmup: throughput harness design notes

Purpose and scope:

This note documents an alternative throughput harness approach we experimented with: a Poisson/Burst interarrival load generator combined with an adaptive warmup/priming phase. Goal is to get stable, repeatable throughput numbers while exercising realistic burstiness patterns and ensuring internal caches (KV, kernel caches, cuBLAS heuristics) and model memory paths are representative of production. This is not the final canonical protocol but captures observations, configuration recommendations, and acceptance criteria for repeatable bench runs.

Why a Poisson + burst model?
- Real traffic is rarely perfectly fixed-interval or completely uniform. Poisson arrivals more closely approximate user-driven API traffic for many product use cases, and explicit short bursts model microburst behavior from multiplexed frontends or sudden fan-out.
- Fixed-interval generators re...

Occurred at: 1741867200000
Content:
Audit log: emit quantization profile change events (route enable/disable + profile ref diffs)

This PR adds structured audit log events for route-level quantization profile changes. We currently persist route config changes and have an audit log primitive, but quantization profile settings were not emitted with enough structure to support compliance exports, internal forensics, and Console change history views. This change emits explicit events when (a) quantization is enabled/disabled at the route level and (b) quantization_profile_ref changes (including transitions to/from "default"). Events include actor identity, tenant + route identifiers, the resolved change type, and a normalized diff payload so downstream exporters/SIEM can index reliably.

Scope:
- Emit new audit event types for quant profile changes in the route config write path.
- Include actor (user/service), auth context, tenant_id, route_id, and change diff fields.
- Normalize diff schema to support both API and ...

Content:
Add feature flag gates for route experiments (beta enablement + rollback)

Summary: Introduces first-class feature flag gates for Route-level A/B testing (“route experiments”) across the control plane and data plane so we can: (1) enable beta accounts safely (allowlist), (2) do region-by-region rollout, and (3) have a single kill switch for emergency rollback. This PR does not change experiment semantics; it only gates evaluation + API surfacing. Context/why: As we expand beta (Phase 2) we need a deterministic, auditable enablement mechanism that does not require config churn or code deploys. We also want to avoid partial enablement (Console shows experiments but router ignores them, or vice versa). This PR aligns gating across: policy engine evaluator, router request context, and Console APIs. FalconBank security review focused on change control for the opt-in Quantization Profile Library. Customer asked how Redwood enforces RBAC for route-level enablement, what gets written to audit logs (fields, retention, export), whether quantization can be globally disabled, and how Redwood prevents silent profile changes. Redwood confirmed: (1) route-level toggles only (no default-on), (2) deterministic resolution with compatibility guardrails and safe fallback to baseline, (3) immutable, versioned profile IDs (with signing/integrity checks) and no silent upgrades when pinning a specific profile, (4) audit events with actor, route, before/after diff, and outcome (applied/fallback/disabled), (5) global kill-switch + per-tenant override option, and (6) Console change history view and API-driven configuration for internal approvals.

Customer requests: zero retention of prompt/response content

# Purpose
Track customers/prospects that require zero retention of prompt/response *content* (inputs/outputs) to ensure consistent implementation and contractual language.

This page is limited to Security/Compliance, Support leadership, and Sales leadership due to customer-sensitive requirements.

# Definition
**Zero retention of content** means: prompt/response bodies are not written to logs, traces, support tooling, or storage systems at rest.

**Allowed (typical):** operational metadata such as request id, timestamps, status codes, latency, model id, region, and aggregated token counts needed for billing and reliability.

Content:
Audit log: emit quantization profile change events (route enable/disable + profile ref diffs)

This PR adds structured audit log events for route-level quantization profile changes. We currently persist route config changes and have an audit log primitive, but quantization profile settings were not emitted with enough structure to support compliance exports, internal forensics, and Console change history views. This change emits explicit events when (a) quantization is enabled/disabled at the route level and (b) quantization_profile_ref changes (including transitions to/from "default"). Events include actor identity, tenant + route identifiers, the resolved change type, and a normalized diff payload so downstream exporters/SIEM can index reliably.

Scope:
- Emit new audit event types for quant profile changes in the route config write path.
- Include actor (user/service), auth context, tenant_id, route_id, and change diff fields.
- Normalize diff schema to support both API and ...

This PR does not change experiment semantics; it only gates evaluation + API surfacing. This PRD defines: (1) the customer problem and target personas, (2) functional requirements for the platform, API Gateway, control plane, and Console, (3) success metrics and guardrails, and (4) a phased release plan with migration and compatibility constraints.

## Problem statement
Redwood’s existing authorization model (RBAC v1 and/or mixed service-local checks) is insufficient for enterprise buyers who require:
- Least-privileg...

Customer requests: zero retention of prompt/response content

# Purpose
Track customers/prospects that require zero retention of prompt/response *content* (inputs/outputs) to ensure consistent implementation and contractual language.

This page is limited to Security/Compliance, Support leadership, and Sales leadership due to customer-sensitive requirements.

# Definition
**Zero retention of content** means: prompt/response bodies are not written to logs, traces, support tooling, or storage systems at rest.

**Allowed (typical):** operational metadata such as request id, timestamps, status codes, latency, model id, region, and aggregated token counts needed for billing and reliability.

This page defines the migration plan from RBAC v1 (coarse-grained roles and implicit access paths) to RBAC v2 (fine-grained permissions with explicit scopes and deny-by-default enforcement). The plan is designed to:

- Prevent customer breakage during staged rollout (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).
- Provide deterministic authorization behavior with measurable deltas between v1 and v2.
- Offer safe rollback paths and clear operator workflows.

recall.text:
We’ve got a Dedicated customer asking (1) what “incident type” we’re calling today’s event and (2) whether the remediation SLA applies to them / what we can commit to.

Symptoms on their side: burst of `429` + some `5xx` spikes for ~12 min, started after they pushed a traffic ramp. We also saw elevated queue depth + GPU saturation on the dedicated pool.
What should I tell them as the incident type, and do we cite the remediation SLA clock?

Owen Phillips: +1, customer is explicitly asking “is this a Capacity incident or a Routing incident?” and wants something they can paste into their internal report.

Rachel Kim: For external comms: don’t use the internal taxonomy labels verbatim (“Capacity/Fleet Shortage”) unless they specifically ask for it. Translate it to plain language: “capacity saturation on your dedicated cluster leadin...

Content:
Change Management Policy for Model-Version and Runtime Changes

h1. Purpose
This policy defines the minimum change-management requirements for production changes that affect model versions, routing policies, serving runtime configuration, or customer-facing inference behavior.

h1. Scope
This applies to all production environments across:
* Hosted API
* Dedicated capacity
* Private deployments (VPC and on-prem)

Examples in-scope:
* Introducing a new model version behind an existing model alias
* Changing default routing/fallback policy
* Changing runtime settings that alter batching/caching/quantization
* Adjusting context-length limits or tool-calling behavior

Out of scope (but still recommended): documentation-only updates and non-production experiments in isolated sandboxes.

h1. Change classes
* Class 0 (Low risk): no customer traffic, no routing changes, no behavioral change (e.g., internal benchmarking).
* Class 1 (Moderate risk): canary t...

Content:
Private Deployments: Upgrade Go/No-Go Checklist (Enterprise)

## Purpose
This checklist is the required go/no-go gate for upgrading an *enterprise* Redwood Private deployment (VPC or on-prem). It defines the minimum validations, evidence, and sign-offs required before starting the maintenance window, and the stop/rollback criteria during execution.

## Scope
Applies to any customer production Private environment upgrade of: (a) control plane components (operator, controller, API gateway), (b) serving runtime/data plane images, (c) cluster dependencies shipped by Redwood (ingress, cert-manager bundles, logging agents), or (d) installer/upgrade tooling.

> Policy dependencies: This page is the operational gate, but approvals/comms requirements are defined in the Change Management Policy and Planned Maintenance Comms SOP. Security-specific sign-off requirements are defined in Secure SDLC: Security Sign-off for Customer Deployments. SLO/error budget...

This page defines the **mandatory security controls** and **approval gates** for enabling **customer-managed audit log export** from Redwood Inference to customer-controlled cloud storage (e.g., S3/GCS/Azure Blob) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

This is a control surface that touches: customer data boundaries, identity and access, encryption, and retention. **Do not enable export** without satisfying the requirements below.

# Scope
In scope:
- Export of **platform audit logs** (auth events, RBAC changes, API key lifecycle, console/admin actions, private control-plane actions).
- Delivery to a **customer-owned destination**.

Out of scope:
- Model inference payload logging (prompt/completion content). That remains disabled by default and requires a separate data logging exception.

Content:
Session‑Resilient Hedge and Recovery Protocol for Hosted Edge

Problem: Our hosted API edge must remain usable during region outages and transient capacity spikes without dropping live streaming sessions or violating tail latency SLOs. Current failover mechanics perform a blunt global cutover which either causes streaming connections to be reset or allows sustained latency/cost regressions when load is shifted to under-provisioned regions. We also lack an integrated, session-aware circuit that can selectively shed load while preserving long‑running conversations and avoiding partial history loss.

Goals:
- Implement a hedging + staged recovery protocol that preserves session continuity for streaming and sessionful requests across active-active regions.
- Provide per-route, per-customer SLO mapping to automated decisions (hedge, shed, divert).
- Add an observable, telemetry-gated circuit that performs graceful degradation rather than abrupt resets...

Actor: rohit-singh
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Intake priority hydrator for console metrics ingest to reduce hot-key latency

Background: The console realtime ingest is experiencing high tail latency driven by a small subset of tenants (hot-key patterns) and bursty admin routes. Current batching and shard routing are reactive and cause amplified queueing for latency-sensitive traffic (dashboard reads, live traces). This ticket introduces an "intake priority hydrator": a lightweight pre-processor in the ingest path that tags and routes incoming events by computed priority, enriches stream metadata for downstream processors, and applies fast-path adjustments (dedicated short-lived queues, early KV cache lookups) to keep tail latency bounded without sacrificing overall throughput.

Actor: mateo-alvarez
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
FP8 signal stabilizer: layer-gated activation, hysteresis, and calibration playbook for Hopper E4M3/E5M2

Problem: Enabling FP8 (E4M3/E5M2) on Hopper for production traffic has produced transient accuracy regressions tied to per-layer signal variance and bursty activation patterns. Current global-profile activation causes noisy layer-level tails to flip quant modes, producing non-deterministic regressions across batches. Goal: Implement a 'signal stabilizer' that (1) performs per-layer gating with short-term hysteresis to avoid flip-flopping during bursts, (2) integrates a canary sampling pipeline that exercises representative production prompts for golden-seed regression checks, and (3) wires calibration snapshots + automatic fallback to INT8/FP16 variants when stability thresholds are breached. This ticket scopes implementation, validation, observability, and a rollout plan for safe FP8 activation on Hopper with clear rollback paths.

Source event id: activity-eng-78123-193d5a5a29f2
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-78123
Actor: ava-chen
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
EventFlow lease fencing and graceful degradation for console metrics pipeline

Problem: The real-time console metrics pipeline can see short-lived duplicate consumers and partition flaps during deploys or autoscaling events. When a consumer restarts or two instances briefly claim the same shard, we observe duplicated increments in tenant metrics, checkpoint races that move offsets backward, and transient spikes in per-tenant token billing. Current leases are optimistic and rely on external consensus which is brittle during GC pauses and cloud flakiness.

Goal: Add a robust lease-fencing layer and a graceful-degradation strategy so that shard handoffs are deterministic, duplicates are bounded, and noisy tenant storms do not violate SLOs. The feature should be compatible with the existing Kafka-like stream and our checkpointing model, support multi-tenant fairness, and allow staged rollout with observability hooks.

Actor: miguel-torres
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Session‑Resilient Hedge and Recovery Protocol for Hosted Edge

Problem: Our hosted API edge must remain usable during region outages and transient capacity spikes without dropping live streaming sessions or violating tail latency SLOs. Current failover mechanics perform a blunt global cutover which either causes streaming connections to be reset or allows sustained latency/cost regressions when load is shifted to under-provisioned regions. We also lack an integrated, session-aware circuit that can selectively shed load while preserving long‑running conversations and avoiding partial history loss.

Goals:
- Implement a hedging + staged recovery protocol that preserves session continuity for streaming and sessionful requests across active-active regions.
- Provide per-route, per-customer SLO mapping to automated decisions (hedge, shed, divert).
- Add an observable, telemetry-gated circuit that performs graceful degradation rather than abrupt resets...

Source event id: activity-eng-214999-4d2f68007861

Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-214999
Actor: rohit-singh
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Intake priority hydrator for console metrics ingest to reduce hot-key latency

Background: The console realtime ingest is experiencing high tail latency driven by a small subset of tenants (hot-key patterns) and bursty admin routes. Current batching and shard routing are reactive and cause amplified queueing for latency-sensitive traffic (dashboard reads, live traces). This ticket introduces an "intake priority hydrator": a lightweight pre-processor in the ingest path that tags and routes incoming events by computed priority, enriches stream metadata for downstream processors, and applies fast-path adjustments (dedicated short-lived queues, early KV cache lookups) to keep tail latency bounded without sacrificing overall throughput.

Content:
urgent: stabilize per-model priming oscillations through resilient microbatch handshake and hedged warmup

Summary: Multiple customers across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments reported intermittent first-token latency spikes and reduced tokens/sec during priming-heavy workloads after last runtime rollout. Symptom profile: repeated 200–900ms TTFT spikes for short sessions (<=50 tokens) and sustained tokens/sec drop by ~25% under mixed prompt-length workloads. Observed higher failure rate for sessions that arrive concurrently with model-variant probing and background KV prefetch tasks.

Investigation highlights:
- Reproduced in staging using a synthetic fanout workload with small context windows and frequent model-variant checks.
- Telemetry showed microbatch handshake races between coalescer and worker when warmup/priming I/O coincided with variant probe traffic.
- Root cause: race in microbatch handshake state machine that allowed inflight priming requests to block worker availabi...

Content:
Probabilistic demotion policy for tier routing with SLA hedging and quota-aware prioritization

Goal: Implement a probabilistic demotion admission controller for tier-based routing that (1) respects per-tenant and per-plan quotas, (2) hedges SLAs for enterprise customers by reserving capacity, and (3) applies soft, probabilistic demotions for free and paid tiers instead of abrupt hard rejections.

Motivation: Current tier routing uses deterministic demotions and strict spillbacks which cause noisy failures and poor UX for bursty free-tier workloads. We want a smoother degradation curve that preserves enterprise SLAs, reduces tail latency spikes, and improves fairness across tenants during capacity pressure.

Design summary:
- Admission model: compute an admission probability p in [0,1] per request based on: remaining quota fraction (EWMA over consumption rate), recent latency headroom, and plan priority multiplier.
- SLA hedging: keep a soft-capacity reservoir (configurable perc...

Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-913577
Actor: mateo-alvarez
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
FP8 signal stabilizer: layer-gated activation, hysteresis, and calibration playbook for Hopper E4M3/E5M2

Problem: Enabling FP8 (E4M3/E5M2) on Hopper for production traffic has produced transient accuracy regressions tied to per-layer signal variance and bursty activation patterns. Current global-profile activation causes noisy layer-level tails to flip quant modes, producing non-deterministic regressions across batches. Goal: Implement a 'signal stabilizer' that (1) performs per-layer gating with short-term hysteresis to avoid flip-flopping during bursts, (2) integrates a canary sampling pipeline that exercises representative production prompts for golden-seed regression checks, and (3) wires calibration snapshots + automatic fallback to INT8/FP16 variants when stability thresholds are breached. This ticket scopes implementation, validation, observability, and a rollout plan for safe FP8 activation on Hopper with clear rollback paths.

Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Audit log ingest & storage playbook: compact indexing, partition tiers, and cost controls

Define and implement a production-ready playbook for ingesting, indexing, partitioning, and retaining audit logs (RBAC v2 events, SSO/SAML auth flows, admin actions). Goals: keep storage costs predictable, ensure fast queryability for recent events, and meet compliance retention rules (per-tenant and global). Deliverables: ingestion pipeline schema and throttling rules, index/partition naming and rollup TTLs, cold-storage export process, deletion/retention automation, and monitoring/runbooks for cost/regression. This ticket focuses on operational patterns and automation rather than the UI for viewing logs.

Content:
Streamed call provenance telemetry and sanitizer orchestration

Problem:
We currently emit coarse-grained streaming telemetry that does not tie individual output chunks to provenance (which tool call produced it, which function invocation context, or the structured-output fragment). This creates three problems: (1) debugging tool/function call failures across progressive delivery is hard because logs/metrics are not correlated to streaming fragments, (2) redaction is inconsistent across partial outputs (sensitive tokens may be emitted and later redacted but traces and stored logs still leak), and (3) backward compatibility with legacy clients that expect event types for incremental outputs complicates instrumentation.

Goal:
Add a lightweight per-chunk provenance and span model plus a sanitizer orchestration layer that ensures logs/metrics exported from streaming paths are correlated, minimally invasive (low latency/CPU), and compatible with older...

Content:
Idempotent consumption ledger and retry-safe metering for low-latency ingestion

Problem: At-least-once delivery patterns from the serving runtime and client SDK retries are causing duplicate billing events in edge cases (network timeouts, infra retries, client replays). Current dedupe mechanisms are insufficient when requests are proxied through gateways or when clients re-use short-lived idempotency keys. Goal: implement a retry-safe, low-latency metering approach that guarantees no double-billing for logically identical requests while preserving SLOs for latency and throughput

Scope: This ticket covers the design and implementation of a sticky consumption ledger that coordinates provisional debits, final commits, and background reconciliation across the ingest, metering, and billing pipelines. Content:
Modular VPC Provisioner: exchangeable subnet profiles, smart-egress adapters, and rolling-upgrade safety net

Create a reusable Terraform module (and companion policy/automation) for provisioning VPC networking for Private deployments. The module must support: multiple subnet profiles (control/data/infra), pluggable egress adapters (NAT gateway, managed egress appliance, IGW), region-aware VPC endpoints, intent-driven routing tables, and a rolling upgrade path with automated health checks and safe fallback. Goal: enable customers to deploy private Redwood clusters with minimal templates while giving platform teams safe upgrade primitives and observable health signals.

Problem: Short-duration high-impact deviations (spikes or drops) in token usage, billing spikes, and tail latency are often lost in aggregate alerts or buried by noisy detectors. We need a backend pipeline + orchestrator that detects variance 'hotspots' across dimensions (customer, route, region, model, deployment tier), computes a compact attribution, and then makes a notification decision that balances noise, operational cost, and customer impact.

Goal: Implement a Variance Hotspot Finder (VHF) that complements existing anomaly systems by focusing on transient, high-variance events and an accompanying Notification Orchestrator that applies adaptive suppression, routing, and escalation rules. The feature should integrate with the Console (dashboards, per-route timelines), existing telemetry storage, and Pager/Slack routing.

Content:
Compatibility offboarding and dependency mitigation roadmap

Run a staged, metrics-driven program to retire legacy compatibility layers for the /v1/compat API surface while minimizing disruption for customers that have indirect dependencies (SDKs, third-party wrappers, cached clients). This ticket coordinates launch planning, release-note sequencing, SDK and docs updates, customer outreach, and support runbooks. The goal is to provide clear upgrade paths, automated detection of at-risk customers, and safe fallbacks that allow the retirement to complete with <0.5% live-customer errors attributable to the change after 60 days.

Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Overflow absorption policy and deferred-charge metering

Goal: introduce an "overflow absorption" capability for Dedicated capacity that temporarily absorbs short-lived traffic spikes into a soft buffer (absorption credits) and defers explicit overage charges. This feature aims to reduce noisy overage alerts, improve customer experience during transient traffic events, and provide predictable reconciliation for billing. The implementation will add a short-lived credit bucket per tenant, control planes for policy, and reconciliation metering that converts absorbed usage into deferred charges subject to caps and decay rules.

Source event id: activity-eng-27183-e3aa50671a33

Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-27183
Actor: mateo-alvarez
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Robust HTTP Event Reassembly and Backoff Protocol for Streaming Responses

Problem: Clients intermittently lose SSE connections during long-running responses (tool calls, structured JSON partials). Current reconnect behavior sometimes produces duplicate function-call side effects, garbled partial JSON, or stalls when the server switches model backends. Goal: Define and implement a robust HTTP event reassembly protocol and client-server backoff strategy that provides: idempotent resume semantics, clear chunk boundaries for structured outputs and function-call transcripts, graceful partial-output rehydration, and a compatibility header that enables gradual rollout across hosted/dedicated/private deployments.

Source event id: activity-eng-78123-193d5a5a29f2
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-78123
Actor: ava-chen
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
EventFlow lease fencing and graceful degradation for console metrics pipeline

Problem: The real-time console metrics pipeline can see short-lived duplicate consumers and partition flaps during deploys or autoscaling events. When a consumer restarts or two instances briefly claim the same shard, we observe duplicated increments in tenant metrics, checkpoint races that move offsets backward, and transient spikes in per-tenant token billing. Current leases are optimistic and rely on external consensus which is brittle during GC pauses and cloud flakiness.

Goal: Add a robust lease-fencing layer and a graceful-degradation strategy so that shard handoffs are deterministic, duplicates are bounded, and noisy tenant storms do not violate SLOs. The feature should be compatible with the existing Kafka-like stream and our checkpointing model, support multi-tenant fairness, and allow staged rollout with observability hooks.

---

Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-87321
Actor: diego-martinez
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Per-layer entropy-aware KV calibration and runtime precision gating for INT8/FP8

Run a targeted experiment that (1) calibrates per-layer KV cache quantization parameters using an entropy-aware objective, (2) validates INT8 and FP8 profiles across diverse workloads, and (3) evaluates a lightweight runtime precision gating mechanism that selects per-request per-layer precision to balance p99 latency and downstream quality. Goal: find a deployment-ready calibration + gating configuration that reduces token cost/latency for conversational workloads while keeping end-user quality regressions within acceptable bounds.

Source event id: activity-eng-210451-ac30db45418e
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-210451
Actor: ravi-patel
Occurred at: 1739880000000
Content:
Safeguarded version orchestrator for private control plane upgrades

Goal: implement a conservative, observable, and reversible control-plane version orchestration pipeline for Private (VPC) deployments. This ticket scopes the orchestration glue, Terraform/IaC validators, and runtime health gates for automated canary promotions across tenant cohorts. The solution must be usable by both Redwood-managed and customer-managed Private control planes and integrate with existing terraform modules and the controlplane operator. Key principles: non-disruptive dry runs, shadow promotion, tenant fencing, telemetry-driven gating, explicit manual approvals for high-risk tenants, and robust rollback/playbook artifacts.

Content:
Unified fetch primitives: cursor semantics, resumable streams, and graceful client downgrade

This PRD defines a cross-language client contract for fetch primitives used by Redwood SDKs: strong cursor pagination, resumable streaming iterators, and downgrade-safe behaviors for older clients. The goal is to provide a single, well-specified surface that SDKs (Python/TS/Go) can implement while preserving backwards compatibility for existing consumers. We want clear failure/retry semantics, an opaque cursor format with compatibility guarantees, and a resumable streaming API that can survive transient infra restarts or route failovers.

- This is intentionally terse: copy into a live Google Sheet for daily updates.

1) Canary (0-5% traffic, 2 hours) — focus on error spike, latency tail, model regressions on eval set.

Version handshake protocol for preserved IO signatures

Burst orchestration policy for dedicated fleets and graceful overage handling

Resilience audit: cross-region amplification during failover exposed circuit-breaker asymmetry

Summary: During an automated cross-region failover triggered by a control-plane maintenance window (2026-03-01 02:00 UTC), traffic reshaping caused an amplification effect in the secondary region. The amplification overran circuit breaker thresholds and caused sustained SLO misses for tail latency (95th/99th). This ticket consolidates the investigation findings, temporary mitigations, and a prioritized set of follow-up action items to prevent recurrence and restore SLO headroom.

Scope: Platform serving runtime (request routing, circuit-breakers), control-plane failover orchestration, and regional autoscaling/load-shedding integration.

Coordinated Sunset Workflow: Automated Notices, Pin Recommendations, and Rollback Paths for Legacy Model Versions

Warp-prefetch slice heuristics for FlashAttention with paged KV

Objective:
This note captures an exploratory design and a small set of heuristics for a warp-cooperative prefetch + slice scheduling strategy targeting FlashAttention-style fused attention kernels when used with a paged KV cache. The goal is to reduce tail latency for long-context chat workloads while keeping per-token cost bounded (i.e., avoid full KV warming), and expose simple knobs for runtime autoscaling/batching decisions.

Context / problem statement:
- Production workload: many short requests interleaved with occasional long-roll-forward chat sessions that read large KV histories.
- Serving runtime uses paged KV: KV entries are sharded into fixed-size slabs (pages) evicted to host/DRAM when GPU memory is constrained.
- FlashAttention fused kernels are high-throughput for dense attention but expect contiguous memory for Q/K/V. Paged KV causes non-contiguous accesses and spikes ...

Actor: mateo-ruiz
Occurred at: 1772193600000
Content:
Cross-proxy normalization of idempotency header causing duplicate tool/function executions

Customer reports repeated duplicate side-effecting tool/function invocations when using function-calling with retries. The customer is on a dedicated plan behind a corporate reverse proxy that normalizes headers (lowercasing, truncation of long header values, and replacing spaces with hyphens). NimbusAI sees duplicate webhook/tool calls on retry even though they supply stable idempotency keys in the SDK. Hypothesis: the proxy is rewriting/persistently normalizing the Idempotency header in-flight, so Redwood's dedupe layer receives a different header value on the retry path and treats it as a new request.

Occurred at: 1772193600000
Content:
Warp-prefetch slice heuristics for FlashAttention with paged KV

Objective:
This note captures an exploratory design and a small set of heuristics for a warp-cooperative prefetch + slice scheduling strategy targeting FlashAttention-style fused attention kernels when used with a paged KV cache. The goal is to reduce tail latency for long-context chat workloads while keeping per-token cost bounded (i.e., avoid full KV warming), and expose simple knobs for runtime autoscaling/batching decisions.

Context / problem statement:
- Production workload: many short requests interleaved with occasional long-roll-forward chat sessions that read large KV histories.
- Serving runtime uses paged KV: KV entries are sharded into fixed-size slabs (pages) evicted to host/DRAM when GPU memory is constrained.
- FlashAttention fused kernels are high-throughput for dense attention but expect contiguous memory for Q/K/V. Paged KV causes non-contiguous accesses and spikes ...

Content:
Cross-proxy normalization of idempotency header causing duplicate tool/function executions

Customer reports repeated duplicate side-effecting tool/function invocations when using function-calling with retries. The customer is on a dedicated plan behind a corporate reverse proxy that normalizes headers (lowercasing, truncation of long header values, and replacing spaces with hyphens). NimbusAI sees duplicate webhook/tool calls on retry even though they supply stable idempotency keys in the SDK. Hypothesis: the proxy is rewriting/persistently normalizing the Idempotency header in-flight, so Redwood's dedupe layer receives a different header value on the retry path and treats it as a new request.

Actor: vikram-singh
Occurred at: 1770897600000
Content:
Deterministic request fingerprinting + bucketing for shadow traffic

Problem
Shadow traffic requires comparing control vs shadow outputs with low variance across time windows. If we use per-request randomness, the set of shadowed requests changes minute-to-minute and makes quality deltas noisy. We need deterministic bucketing so that the same request (as defined by stable inputs) is consistently mirrored (or not) for the lifetime of an experiment / policy.

Goal
Implement stable, privacy-preserving request fingerprinting and deterministic bucketing that:
- Produces consistent sampling decisions across time windows and retries.
- Supports multiple concurrent shadow experiments on the same route without cross-contamination.
- Minimizes PII risk (no raw prompts in hashes; no reversible identifiers).
- Allows per-experiment sampling % and per-variant assignment where needed.
- Works for Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments with consistent semantics.

Content:
Stage Spectral Cohort Insight

Purpose
This doc is a compact results-and-insights brief from a cross-cohort end-to-end tracing exercise run across model variants and hardware types. Goal: explain where p50/p95/p99 comes from at the per-stage level, surface unusual spectral modes (multi-modal latency shapes), and recommend targeted mitigations we can experiment with in the next sprint.

Background and motivation
We observed an uptick in p99 across customer traffic the week of 2026-01-28. Host-level metrics (CPU/GPU util) were unimpressive; traces showed long tail stages that vary by request cohort. This investigation stitched together 45k sampled traces (1% sampling of production ingress) across two representative fleets: A100-backed gpt-medium-8k-fp16 and H100-backed llamaX-70B-q4. Traces include full stage timing (ingress proxy, auth/token, cache lookup, batching wait, model forward, kv-write, postprocess/serializ...

Actor: ravi-k-patel
Occurred at: 1770897600000
Content:
SLI curve fitting and dashboard experiment notes

Summary:
This is a working doc for an experiment to: (1) define compact, cost-aware SLIs that reflect user-perceived latency and model-quality risk; (2) try a curve-fitting approach to map metric distributions into an SLI curve instead of brittle thresholds; (3) prototype a small dashboard surface and trace anchors for efficient investigation. The goal is a trial during the next canary rollout (Q1 release window).

Why curve-fitting?
- Threshold SLIs (p95 < 200ms) are easy but brittle for multitenant inference with bursty token flows and KV cache effects.
- We want an SLI that degrades smoothly and is interpretable for oncall and product stakeholders, and that can be tied to token-cost implications.

Goals for the experiment

Goal
Define customer-facing messaging for the KV cache compaction + eviction work landing in runtime-1.22. This ticket is specifically about: (1) what customers should expect to improve, (2) how we describe it in plain language without over-promising, and (3) tier/config implications for Hosted vs Dedicated (and what Support/CS should say during rollout).

Context (problem we’re solving)
Some customers see burst-driven p95/p99 latency spikes and occasional OOM/request shedding in sustained decode workloads with mixed sequence lengths. Actor: vikram-singh
Occurred at: 1770897600000
Content:
Deterministic request fingerprinting + bucketing for shadow traffic

Problem
Shadow traffic requires comparing control vs shadow outputs with low variance across time windows. If we use per-request randomness, the set of shadowed requests changes minute-to-minute and makes quality deltas noisy. We need deterministic bucketing so that the same request (as defined by stable inputs) is consistently mirrored (or not) for the lifetime of an experiment / policy.

Goal
Implement stable, privacy-preserving request fingerprinting and deterministic bucketing that:
- Produces consistent sampling decisions across time windows and retries.
- Supports multiple concurrent shadow experiments on the same route without cross-contamination.
- Minimizes PII risk (no raw prompts in hashes; no reversible identifiers).
- Allows per-experiment sampling % and per-variant assignment where needed.
- Works for Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments with consistent semantics.

Non-g...

Source app: confluence

question: How did runtime latency, routing, and dedicated capacity decisions connect across teams?
answer:
**How the three “speed‑knobs” – runtime latency, routing, and Dedicated‑capacity – are tied together across the organization** | Decision area | Who owns the “go‑to‑market” or engineering artifact | What they decide | How the decision is handed off to the other two areas | |---|---|---|---| | **Runtime latency** | • **Runtime engineering** – Mateo Alvarez (runtime arch), Noah Patel (runtime arch), Hiro Tanaka (kernel), Sofia Ivanova (scheduling)
• **SRE** – Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Allison Grant (metrics & SLIs)
• **Product** – Marissa Cole (PM‑912 LTS requirements) | • Target p95 / p99 latency budgets per‑model/region (see *Runtime LTS PRD* and *Adaptive latency‑budget ticket* **ENG‑778213**)
• Required observability metrics (pre‑check, decode‑time, error‑rate) – see *Metric‑census playbook* (Ava Martinez) | • The latency budget is exposed as a **policy constant** that the routing engine reads (see *Pragmatic routing lattice* and *Control‑plane DSL*).
• SRE dashboards flag a breach; the incident‑orchestration matrix (authored by **Evan Park**) automatically triggers a **routing fallback** or a **Dedicated‑capacity surge**. | | **Routing** | • **Platform/Control‑plane** – Ethan Park (policy engine), Diego Alvarez (routing‑lattice), Ava Chen (routing‑lattice notes)
• **Security/RBAC** – Naomi Feldman (RBAC‑v2 matrix)
• **Console** – Priya Nair (admin‑wizard) | • Multi‑region, latency‑first vs cost‑first routing rules (the *routing lattice* document)
• Per‑tenant latency budget enforcement via **capacity‑tokens** (ENG‑778213)
• Hard‑deny precedence defined in *ADR: rollout‑policy evaluation order* (Jordan Lee) | • When the routing engine sees a latency‑budget violation it selects the **next‑best variant** (cheaper or faster) and also emits a *capacity‑token* request to the Dedicated‑capacity subsystem.
• The console surface (Upgrade‑wizard, *Private Console* pages) shows the auto‑fallback path to the operator and records the decision for audit. | | **Dedicated‑capacity** | • **Product – Dedicated** – Talia Benson (throughput‑SLO tiering), Ava Chen (Adaptive Burst Arbiter PRD)
• **Capacity Engineering** – Marco Ruiz (Flow‑guard‑rail), Rafael Ortega (Adaptive‑burst arbiter)
• **Finance / GTM** – Lauren Bishop (tier pricing) | • Reserved baseline throughput per‑tenant (see *Dedicated‑throughput SLO + tiers* and *throughput‑SLO workshop* notes)
• On‑demand burst credits via the **Gradual Burst Gateway** (Priya Natarajan)
• Capacity‑token pricing &‑usage limits (see *Adaptive latency budgets & capacity tokens* ticket) | • The routing engine consumes a **capacity‑token** before sending a request to a region; if the token pool is exhausted the router falls back to a cheaper/faster model (or to a different region).
• If latency metrics cross the SLO, the Dedicated‑capacity team auto‑expands the token bucket (burst gateway) or upgrades the tenant’s reservation – this is coordinated through the **Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator** (PM‑100999) so that the runtime version, routing config, and capacity reservation are rolled out together. | | **Cross‑team coordination / process** | • **Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator (DRTO)** – PM‑100999 (Priya Natarajan)
• **Sprint‑Orchestra & Fallback‑Lane Runbook** – PM‑631001 (Diego Alvarez)
• **Runtime Incident Orchestration & Capacity Decision Matrix** – authored by **Evan Park** | • Provides a single source‑of‑truth calendar for runtime patches, routing‑policy rollouts, and Dedicated‑capacity upgrades.
• Defines go/no‑go gates (latency regression, routing‑policy validation, capacity‑token health) and the escalation path (SRE → Platform → Product). | • When a runtime change lands (e.g., a new kernel in the LTS channel), DRTO checks that the **routing lattice** has been re‑compiled with the new latency constants and that **Dedicated‑capacity** has enough token head‑room for the expected traffic.
• If any gate fails, the runbook forces a rollback (see *Private Upgrade Flight Controller* – ENG‑18540) and automatically re‑opens the capacity‑token pool to the previous size. | ### Quick flow‑chart (textual) 1. **Latency monitor** (SRE) → breach? → **Routing engine** reads latency budget → selects next‑best variant **and** requests a **capacity‑token**. 2. **Routing engine** (Platform) → token granted? → forward request; else **fallback** to lower‑cost model **or** signal Dedicated‑capacity for a **burst grant**. 3. **Dedicated‑capacity** (Product/Capacity Eng) → burst granted → token pool refreshed → routing continues; if burst limits hit, **DRTO** schedules a capacity‑reservation expansion (or a tier‑upgrade). 4. All decisions are recorded in the **Console upgrade wizard** and the **audit‑log** (RBAC‑v2 matrix) so the customer can see latency, routing, and capacity changes side‑by‑side. --- **Bottom line:** - **Runtime engineers** set the latency targets and publish the numbers. - **Platform/routing engineers** turn those numbers into per‑tenant routing rules and token‑based admission checks. - **Dedicated‑capacity team** guarantees that enough reserved or burst capacity exists to satisfy the routing decisions and, when needed, expands the reservation. The **Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator**, the **Sprint‑Orchestra runbook**, and the **Runtime Incident Orchestration matrix** are the glue that makes the three knobs move together without stepping on each other’s toes.

recall.text:
view=companies&company=GALV-00421
Actor: lena-park
Occurred at: 1732104000000
Content:
Galvanic Harbor Systems

— Vertical: payments platform (real-time fraud + in-chat agent).
— Primary ask: Dedicated GPU pool with throughput guarantees for peak windows (US region).
— Latency SLO targets: p95 <150ms for <256-token responses, p99 <400ms for longer pipelined flows.
— Throughput planning: target sustained 400–600 rps during business hours, 1,200 rps burst capacity during promos. Need guidance on reserved GPU sizing (estimating 6x A100-equivalents) and burst behavior.

— Queueing behavior: want bounded queue with backpressure signal to app at 250 queued requests; prefer graceful degradation to lower-cost model variant rather than hard reject...

Actor: liam-orourke
Occurred at: 1732104000000
Content:
Profile confidence scoring and progressive rollback for E4M3/E5M2 FP8 enablement

Implement a profile-level confidence scoring system and an automated progressive rollback mechanism to reduce customer impact during FP8 (E4M3/E5M2) rollouts on Hopper. This ticket bundles: (1) a deterministic scoring metric computed from calibration evals and runtime regression probes, (2) a policy engine that maps score bands to rollout actions (continue, pause, shadow, rollback), and (3) integration with canary pipelines and metrics dashboards. The goal is to avoid blunt on/off activation by making profile activation conditional on measurable confidence and to provide a fast, automated path to revert when drift or instabilities are detected.

Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1731931200000
Content:
Untangle batch commit path and migrate KV cache lease ledger out of global scheduler

We have accumulating complexity in the continuous batching controller: commit/ack paths are entangled with the global scheduler and a KV-cache lease/ledger implementation that serializes operations under a global lock. This is causing tail latency spikes under mixed workloads and makes iterative rollouts risky. This ticket aims to separate the batch commit state machine from scheduling responsibilities and migrate the KV-cache lease ledger into a sharded lease coordinator with lease handoff semantics and lock-free read paths.

Occurred at: 1731672000000
Content:
delta-driven pin throttling and tiered reuse allocator for paged KV

Motivation: Our paged KV block manager saw high tail latency and frequent pin-induced stalls when conversational workloads produce short bursts of focused hot prefixes. Existing heuristics pinned aggressively on first-detect hotness and coalesced eagerly, which increased contention and caused kernel scheduling jitter. This change introduces a delta-driven pin heuristic plus a lightweight tiered reuse allocator to smooth pin activity and defer expensive coalesces into the background.

Source event id: activity-emergency-rollback-serving-runtime-hosted--ec9352cb9c6e
Source app: confluence
Source URL: /confluence?space=eng-serving-runtime&page=emergency-rollback-serving-runtime-hosted-dedicated
Actor: hiro-tanaka
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
Emergency rollback: Serving Runtime (Hosted + Dedicated)

h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to perform an *emergency rollback* of the Serving Runtime release across *Hosted* and *Dedicated* fleets. It focuses on restoring SLOs safely and quickly while preserving evidence for later analysis.

h1. When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when one or more is true and mitigation is not available within ~30 minutes:
* p95 *time-to-first-token (TTFT)* regression > 30% in 2+ regions for 10+ minutes
* error rate > 2% on generation routes due to runtime (5xx, timeouts, OOM)
* canary cohort shows sustained degradation and traffic shift cannot be halted cleanly
* repeated GPU OOM or kernel crash loops attributable to the new runtime build

*If unsure*: declare an incident and page Runtime + SRE (see incident process) and start preparing rollback steps in parallel.

h1. Preconditions
* An incident has been declared if customer impact is ...

Source event id: activity-production-deployment-gates-hosted-api-and-ac449b08f3f7
Source app: confluence
Source URL: /confluence?space=architecture-and-standards&page=production-deployment-gates-hosted-api-and-console
Actor: david-kim
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
Production Deployment Gates: Hosted API and Console

h1. Summary
This standard defines the minimum pre-deploy and post-deploy gates for production changes to the *Hosted API* and *Console*. It is intended to be used alongside the relevant runbooks and change management policy.

h1. Applies to
* Hosted API services (request router, inference gateway, streaming gateway, embeddings service, auth edge)
* Console services (console-web, console-api)
* Shared dependencies when changed as part of a release (feature flags, policy configs)

h1. Required release artifacts
For every production release you must be able to provide:
* Build identifier (image digest and/or build number)
* Release notes snippet (even internal-only)
* Link to change record (ticket)
* Link to PR(s) and test results

h1. Pre-deploy gates (all required unless explicitly exempted)
1. *Unit tests:* Green CI for all touched packages.
2. *Integration tests:* Green for service-l...

Source event id: activity-adr-rollout-policy-evaluation-order-and-pr-df02098da9da
Source app: confluence
Source URL: /confluence?space=architecture-and-standards&page=adr-rollout-policy-evaluation-order-and-precedence-hosted-canary-rollouts
Actor: jordan-lee
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
ADR: Rollout policy evaluation order and precedence (Hosted canary rollouts)

## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR defines how **rollout policies** (guardrails, approvals, stop/rollback) compose with existing **routing policies** (fallback chains, region routing / data residency, quotas/rate limits) for **Hosted canary rollouts**. It also defines precedence and deny semantics so the Console, control plane, and data plane behave consistently.

Key decision: we treat policies as a layered system with **hard denies first**, then **routing constraints**, then **rollout split selection**, then **runtime fallback execution**. Explicit deny always wins.

---

## Context
Redwood Hosted routing already supports:
- Route-level model selection and fallback chains (e.g., Model A → Model B)
- Region routing and data residency constraints
- Quotas / rate limits and tenant suspension mechanisms
- Reliability behaviors (timeouts, retries, circuit breakers)

Hosted canary ...

Content:
introduce lazy schema probe with explainable hints and streaming rollback

Motivation: customers integrating structured output (json-schema) want early, actionable feedback when schema mismatches occur in streaming responses but do not want hard failures by default. This PR implements a lightweight "lazy schema probe" that runs a shallow validation on the first N tokens or first structural boundary, emits explainable hint events into the stream and logs, and only triggers a rollback+hard-failure path if the probe detects irrecoverable mismatches and service config requires strict enforcement. Changes: 1) runtime/pkg/schema_probe: new probe implementation that does minimal token/shape checks and returns a list of human-friendly hints (e.g., 'missing field "user_id"', 'expected array but got string at path /items') instead of raw validator dumps; 2) streaming runtime: attach probe to stream start and create check-pointing token (rollback_token) that allows rew...

Actor: lina-park
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
introduce-prefill-sentinel-compactizer-and-cli-for-tenant-econ

Motivation: tenant warmstart and prefill heuristics currently produce suboptimal cache utilization for low-cardinality workloads which inflates per-token cost. This PR adds a lightweight runtime 'prefill sentinel' that compacts low-value KV entries and a companion CLI to synthesize tenant prefill profiles and surface conservative quantization suggestions. The goal is to reduce tail token spend and improve contiguous cache hit rates without changing public API behavior. Implementation summary: added a sentinel compactizer algorithm in the runtime prefill path to detect stale/low-reuse KV prefixes, a tenant-level sampler and scoring function to derive compacting thresholds, a compact-while-warmup mode for Dedicated instances, metrics/tracing hooks for hit-rate impact, and a small CLI tool (prefillctl) that generates recommended batch/cache/quant presets from historical traces. This sing...

Source event id: activity-931212-f5fcea4a5f7f
Source app: github

Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
add-oci-prefetcher-and-kubelet-ephemeral-overlay-for-airgapped-bootstrap

Problem: air-gapped installs often spend excessive time pulling large runtime images and kernel modules during initial bootstrap. Existing delta snapshots help, but cluster bring-up still faces long cold starts and fragile manual registry seeding.

Solution: introduce an OCI prefetcher + index format plus a thin ephemeral kubelet overlay that allows the installer to stream and stage only required image layers and kubelet artifacts into a temporary local store before handing control to the normal runtime. Key components in this PR: (1) installer/terraform module to create a prefetcher DaemonSet and registry proxy for sealed bundles; (2) a compact OCI index format and tooling (installer/tools/oci-indexer) that produces layered prefetch manifests optimized for minimal disk churn; (3) helm chart additions that render a kubelet-ephemeral overlay (charts/redwood-private/templates/kubelet-ep...

PM: Talia Benson
Exec sponsor: Mateo Alvarez (Runtime)
Last updated: 2025-02-03
Status: Draft proposal for ADR approval

Summary
We should introduce a Long Term Support (LTS) channel for the Redwood serving runtime to serve Dedicated and Private customers who need a stable baseline for 6–12+ months. The LTS line will prioritize kernel + scheduling stability, predictable patch cadence, and audited/reproducible artifacts over rapid feature delivery. The main output is a branch strategy, a backport rubric and approvals workflow, a patch cadence with clear expectations, and packaging support (Private installer + Helm pinning) to make upgrades/rollbacks safe.

Why now (customer pain + business impact)

Actor: ava-chen
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
DevOps burden matrix — self-hosted vs managed inference

Purpose: working brief for GTM and AE squads to quickly reason about customer tradeoffs when they ask about self-hosting (vLLM/TGI/TensorRT-LLM stacks). This is meant to be practical — short diagnostics, core objections + counters, and a simple per-persona decision matrix that sales/SEs can use in early discovery.

Executive thesis:
- The buyer decision rarely hinges only on peak latency or best single-request throughput. It is dominated by total operational burden (people + time), risk (security/SLAs/compliance), and the cost of maintaining parity with model innovations.
- Self-hosted stacks (vLLM/TGI/TensorRT-LLM) can win on single-case economics for very large, stable, latency-tolerant workloads where the customer already has mature infra teams and strict on-prem/regulatory needs.
- Managed platforms (Redwood) win when teams prioritize time-to-production, predictable unit economics...

Content:
request-to-token-lattice-scheduler-architectural-notes

Summary:
This note proposes a middle-ground scheduler we call the Request->Token Lattice (RTL) that blends request-level and token-level batching semantics. The goal is to improve throughput and token efficiency while preserving request completion fairness and bounded latency SLOs for mixed workloads (short chats, long generations, embeddings). This doc is intentionally practical: it lists constraints, a runnable core algorithm sketch, configuration knobs, testing/metrics plan, and rollout considerations for Dedicated/Private deployments.

Motivation and problem statement:
- Continuous token-level coalescing maximizes GPU utilization but can penalize short requests and produce outlier tail latencies (token starvation).
- Pure request-level batching preserves request fairness but underutilizes throughput when many requests are short or have dissimilar sequence length distributions.
- E...

Prospective Reserve Allocator and Oscillation Mitigator — PRD (draft)

Summary:
This doc proposes the Prospective Reserve Allocator (PRA): a predictive, short-duration reservation and smoothing controller for Dedicated capacity that reduces oscillation (hot-cold churn), improves fairness for unpredictable workloads, and provides explicit isolation windows for high-priority routes. PRA is targeted at customers on Dedicated who have bursty traffic patterns that cause frequent scale/evict cycles for other customers on the same physical pool.

Problem space and motivating examples:
- Our current Dedicated reservation and prewarm heuristics handle steady growth well but struggle with rapidly shifting demand from short-lived campaigns (ad pushes, feature launches, data migrations). The result: repeated autoscaler churn, degraded latency tail for mid-tier customers, and noisy neighbor complaints.

Content:
Layer-aware microbatching and ring collective scheduling proposal

Summary:

This is a working proposal for a practical multi-GPU serving strategy that combines layer-aware microbatching with an adaptive ring-collective scheduler. Goal: reduce tail latency variability and per-token cost for large autoregressive models under mixed request sizes and heterogeneous hardware interconnects. This doc focuses on three levers: microbatch placement, KV cache partitioning aligned with pipeline/tensor stages, and dynamic NCCL collective selection based on per-layer communication patterns.

Why this problem:
- In production inference we see latency jitter when requests of varying sequence lengths and batch sizes compete for the same GPUs.
- Naive sharding (round-robin or static layer splits) leads to imbalanced NVLink/NCCL utilization: some ranks become bandwidth-starved while others idle.
- KV cache traffic during long-context calls interferes with streaming sma...

Actor: priya-raman
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
sparse-slot-utilization-cuda-mempool-heuristics

Summary:

This is an investigative note about a recurring class of OOMs we see on multi-tenant Dedicated nodes when sequence length or batch shape distribution shifts. Unlike a single catastrophic allocation failure, these OOMs correlate with high overall free memory reported by the CUDA allocator but an inability to fulfill intermediate allocations due to many small unusable free slots ("sparse slot" or "slot fragmentation").

Goal:
- Characterize the allocator-level behavior that creates sparse slots and prevents reuse.
- Validate hypotheses about cudaMallocAsync / CUDA mempool attributes and deferred-free timing.
- Propose mitigation heuristics (pre-reserve slab, size-class coalescer, workspace compaction) that are implementable in the serving runtime.

Observed symptom set (field reports + local repro):
- GPU memory reported by nvidia-smi shows >20% free, yet requests fail with CU...

Content:
Emergency rollback: Serving Runtime (Hosted + Dedicated)

h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to perform an *emergency rollback* of the Serving Runtime release across *Hosted* and *Dedicated* fleets. It focuses on restoring SLOs safely and quickly while preserving evidence for later analysis.

h1. When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when one or more is true and mitigation is not available within ~30 minutes:
* p95 *time-to-first-token (TTFT)* regression > 30% in 2+ regions for 10+ minutes
* error rate > 2% on generation routes due to runtime (5xx, timeouts, OOM)
* canary cohort shows sustained degradation and traffic shift cannot be halted cleanly
* repeated GPU OOM or kernel crash loops attributable to the new runtime build

*If unsure*: declare an incident and page Runtime + SRE (see incident process) and start preparing rollback steps in parallel.

h1. Preconditions
* An incident has been declared if customer impact is ...

h1. Required release artifacts
For every production release you must be able to provide:
* Build identifier (image digest and/or build number)
