Actor: jordan-lee
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
ADR: Rollout policy evaluation order and precedence (Hosted canary rollouts)

## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR defines how **rollout policies** (guardrails, approvals, stop/rollback) compose with existing **routing policies** (fallback chains, region routing / data residency, quotas/rate limits) for **Hosted canary rollouts**. It also defines precedence and deny semantics so the Console, control plane, and data plane behave consistently.

Key decision: we treat policies as a layered system with **hard denies first**, then **routing constraints**, then **rollout split selection**, then **runtime fallback execution**. Explicit deny always wins.

---

## Context
Redwood Hosted routing already supports:
- Route-level model selection and fallback chains (e.g., Model A → Model B)
- Region routing and data residency constraints
- Quotas / rate limits and tenant suspension mechanisms
- Reliability behaviors (timeouts, retries, circuit breakers)

Hosted canary ...

Content:
introduce lazy schema probe with explainable hints and streaming rollback

Motivation: customers integrating structured output (json-schema) want early, actionable feedback when schema mismatches occur in streaming responses but do not want hard failures by default. This PR implements a lightweight "lazy schema probe" that runs a shallow validation on the first N tokens or first structural boundary, emits explainable hint events into the stream and logs, and only triggers a rollback+hard-failure path if the probe detects irrecoverable mismatches and service config requires strict enforcement. Changes: 1) runtime/pkg/schema_probe: new probe implementation that does minimal token/shape checks and returns a list of human-friendly hints (e.g., 'missing field "user_id"', 'expected array but got string at path /items') instead of raw validator dumps; 2) streaming runtime: attach probe to stream start and create check-pointing token (rollback_token) that allows rew...

Actor: lina-park
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
introduce-prefill-sentinel-compactizer-and-cli-for-tenant-econ

Motivation: tenant warmstart and prefill heuristics currently produce suboptimal cache utilization for low-cardinality workloads which inflates per-token cost. This PR adds a lightweight runtime 'prefill sentinel' that compacts low-value KV entries and a companion CLI to synthesize tenant prefill profiles and surface conservative quantization suggestions. The goal is to reduce tail token spend and improve contiguous cache hit rates without changing public API behavior. Implementation summary: added a sentinel compactizer algorithm in the runtime prefill path to detect stale/low-reuse KV prefixes, a tenant-level sampler and scoring function to derive compacting thresholds, a compact-while-warmup mode for Dedicated instances, metrics/tracing hooks for hit-rate impact, and a small CLI tool (prefillctl) that generates recommended batch/cache/quant presets from historical traces. Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1738584000000
Content:
add-oci-prefetcher-and-kubelet-ephemeral-overlay-for-airgapped-bootstrap

Problem: air-gapped installs often spend excessive time pulling large runtime images and kernel modules during initial bootstrap. Existing delta snapshots help, but cluster bring-up still faces long cold starts and fragile manual registry seeding.

Solution: introduce an OCI prefetcher + index format plus a thin ephemeral kubelet overlay that allows the installer to stream and stage only required image layers and kubelet artifacts into a temporary local store before handing control to the normal runtime. Key components in this PR: (1) installer/terraform module to create a prefetcher DaemonSet and registry proxy for sealed bundles; (2) a compact OCI index format and tooling (installer/tools/oci-indexer) that produces layered prefetch manifests optimized for minimal disk churn; (3) helm chart additions that render a kubelet-ephemeral overlay (charts/redwood-private/templates/kubelet-ep...

Summary:
This note proposes a middle-ground scheduler we call the Request->Token Lattice (RTL) that blends request-level and token-level batching semantics. The goal is to improve throughput and token efficiency while preserving request completion fairness and bounded latency SLOs for mixed workloads (short chats, long generations, embeddings). This doc is intentionally practical: it lists constraints, a runnable core algorithm sketch, configuration knobs, testing/metrics plan, and rollout considerations for Dedicated/Private deployments.

Motivation and problem statement:
- Continuous token-level coalescing maximizes GPU utilization but can penalize short requests and produce outlier tail latencies (token starvation).
- Pure request-level batching preserves request fairness but underutilizes throughput when many requests are short or have dissimilar sequence length distributions.

Layer-aware microbatching and ring collective scheduling proposal

Summary:

This is a working proposal for a practical multi-GPU serving strategy that combines layer-aware microbatching with an adaptive ring-collective scheduler. Goal: reduce tail latency variability and per-token cost for large autoregressive models under mixed request sizes and heterogeneous hardware interconnects. This doc focuses on three levers: microbatch placement, KV cache partitioning aligned with pipeline/tensor stages, and dynamic NCCL collective selection based on per-layer communication patterns.

- In production inference we see latency jitter when requests of varying sequence lengths and batch sizes compete for the same GPUs.

Content:
sparse-slot-utilization-cuda-mempool-heuristics

Summary:

This is an investigative note about a recurring class of OOMs we see on multi-tenant Dedicated nodes when sequence length or batch shape distribution shifts. Unlike a single catastrophic allocation failure, these OOMs correlate with high overall free memory reported by the CUDA allocator but an inability to fulfill intermediate allocations due to many small unusable free slots ("sparse slot" or "slot fragmentation").

Goal:
- Characterize the allocator-level behavior that creates sparse slots and prevents reuse.
- Validate hypotheses about cudaMallocAsync / CUDA mempool attributes and deferred-free timing.
- Propose mitigation heuristics (pre-reserve slab, size-class coalescer, workspace compaction) that are implementable in the serving runtime.

Observed symptom set (field reports + local repro):
- GPU memory reported by nvidia-smi shows >20% free, yet requests fail with CU...

The immediate cause was a mismatch between region-prefixed routing rules and recently adjusted circuit-breaker thresholds combined with an aggressive load-shedding policy that targeted secondary regions. That sequence amplified traffic into a single warm region, which then experienced KV-cache cold-start tails and transient saturation. This ticket captures the timeline, root causes, interim mitigations, and an owner-assigned remediation plan to prevent recurrence.

This note outlines a concept for letting tenants negotiate ephemeral burst capacity while keeping strict per-tenant SLAs decomposed into measurable headroom components. Why this idea? (problem we see)
- Customers want predictable baseline capacity but also need predictable, short bursts for launches, demos, batch spikes.

- Operators want clear accountability and easy manual/automated fallbacks when island-level pressure grows.

Content:
Tenant Grace Windows and Scaleback Simulator - PRD Proposal

Summary:

This PRD proposes a new product/ops feature for Dedicated capacity: explicit tenant grace windows plus a scaleback simulator that lets product/ops and SRE evaluate soft-scaleback and preemption policies before they hit customers. The goal is to reduce customer-visible failures during transient spikes while preserving strong isolation guarantees and predictable unit economics.

Why this feels necessary (pain):
- Customers with reserved pools experience occasional short bursts that exceed committed capacity; current options either (a) accept user-facing throttling, (b) over-provision costly extra reserve, or (c) fallback to hosted models with uneven latency.
- Our existing burst/backfill mechanisms are opaque when they trigger and hard for SEs/CS to explain.
- Operations has no controlled way to test different soft-scaleback behaviors (grace window lengths, credit burn rates, ...

Arrival-pattern-sensitive batcher lab notes

This note collects exploratory runs and heuristics for making the continuous-batcher robust to non-stationary arrival patterns (bursty, diurnal, and multimodal) while preserving tail latency SLOs and inter-tenant fairness. Focus knobs: max_batch_size, dynamic_timeout_ms, urgency_decay, prefill_threshold, jitter_seed, and demotion_policy.

- Goal: reduce p99 by ~20% on production-like replay while keeping throughput within 5% of baseline and avoiding starved low-priority tenants.

Actor: priya-nair
Occurred at: 1759406400000
Content:
Python GC + epoll interaction causing bursty latency spikes (investigation notes)

Summary:

Observed periodic tail-latency spikes (~p99-p999) on the hosted API when small bursts of requests arrive (bursts = 50-200 reqs/sec for ~200-800ms). Median and p95 are stable; p99/p999 move dramatically (p99 from ~45ms to 280-420ms depending on load). This note collates profiling evidence pointing to an interaction between Python GC, epoll wait wakeups, and our tokenization/request-parse hot paths.

Context / hypothesis:
- We serve many short requests that are parse/tokenize + model call. Tokenizer is a C-extension but we allocate short-lived Python objects around it for metadata and parsing.
- The server uses a mix of threads (acceptor + io threads) and a ThreadPoolExecutor for blocking preproc (tokenization + request decode).
- We saw that GC collections (non-controversial) coincide with short periods where many worker threads are paused and then wake up simultaneously caus...

Content:
Eval ensemble and triggerable rollback policy for cost-driven config changes

Goal: Build a low-risk, broadly applicable evaluation and rollback system that validates batching/caching/quantization recommendations before they are applied live. The focus is on combining multiple automated quality signals into a single decision score, surfacing targeted human spot-checks for borderline changes, and providing a triggerable, reversible rollback policy tied to observable quality regressions.

Why: Recent cost-tuning experiments yield measurable token-cost savings but occasionally introduce subtle semantic regressions for small customer segments. Purely automated thresholds lead to false positives/negatives; purely manual review is slow. We need a reproducible, auditable harness that balances speed, coverage, and safety for config rollouts across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments.

Content:
Burst reserve negotiation and SLA decomposition — PRD brief

Purpose and summary:

This note outlines a concept for letting tenants negotiate ephemeral burst capacity while keeping strict per-tenant SLAs decomposed into measurable headroom components. Goal: let teams buy or borrow short-duration capacity in a predictable, observable way, avoid noisy-neighbor fallout, and provide explicit operational handoffs when degradation occurs.

Why this idea? (problem we see)
- Customers want predictable baseline capacity but also need predictable, short bursts for launches, demos, batch spikes.
- Current naive burst schemes either (a) over-commit and cause tail latency spikes for co-tenants, or (b) rigidly isolate and waste idle GPUs.
- Operators want clear accountability and easy manual/automated fallbacks when island-level pressure grows.

High-level approach
- Decompose SLA into three measurable tokens: latency budget, throughput headroom, and warm-in...

Actor: priya-nair

Content:
Python GC + epoll interaction causing bursty latency spikes (investigation notes)

Summary:

Observed periodic tail-latency spikes (~p99-p999) on the hosted API when small bursts of requests arrive (bursts = 50-200 reqs/sec for ~200-800ms). Median and p95 are stable; p99/p999 move dramatically (p99 from ~45ms to 280-420ms depending on load). This note collates profiling evidence pointing to an interaction between Python GC, epoll wait wakeups, and our tokenization/request-parse hot paths.

Context / hypothesis:
- We serve many short requests that are parse/tokenize + model call. Tokenizer is a C-extension but we allocate short-lived Python objects around it for metadata and parsing.
- The server uses a mix of threads (acceptor + io threads) and a ThreadPoolExecutor for blocking preproc (tokenization + request decode).

Content:
Tenant-origin routing and locality-aware analytics write watermarks

Problem: The console backend ingest and analytics pipelines currently use a single global write stream for usage events. This causes two operational problems: (1) tenants with region constraints can end up with analytic copies in non-compliant regions during backfill or replay, and (2) global spikes create contagion risks for downstream storage/cost and SLOs. Goal: implement tenant-origin routing with locality-aware write watermarks so that analytic events are written to region-preferring mirrors while maintaining multi-region durability and allowing safe cross-region replay only when explicitly permitted.

Content:
Rollback Procedure: Quantization Profiles (Route-level disable + Global Kill Switch)

h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to safely roll back Quantization Profile Library enablement when we observe quality regressions, latency/cost anomalies, error spikes, or compatibility issues. It covers:

* Disabling quantization at *route level* (preferred, least blast radius)
* Applying *tenant-level override* (if available/required)
* Triggering the *global kill switch* (emergency)
* Identifying impacted tenants/routes and confirming recovery via dashboards, logs, and traces

This runbook is intended for Eng Oncall, SRE, and Platform Control Plane oncall.

h1. Definitions (quick)
* *Quantization profile*: A versioned profile artifact applied by the serving runtime for an eligible model+hardware regime.
* *Route-level enablement*: Route config includes a quantization_profile_ref (or “default”) that can be toggled.
* *Fallback*: Policy engine/runtime chooses baseline precision ...

Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-99111
Actor: diego-alvarez
Occurred at: 1741348800000
Content:
Tenant-origin routing and locality-aware analytics write watermarks

Problem: The console backend ingest and analytics pipelines currently use a single global write stream for usage events. This causes two operational problems: (1) tenants with region constraints can end up with analytic copies in non-compliant regions during backfill or replay, and (2) global spikes create contagion risks for downstream storage/cost and SLOs. Goal: implement tenant-origin routing with locality-aware write watermarks so that analytic events are written to region-preferring mirrors while maintaining multi-region durability and allowing safe cross-region replay only when explicitly permitted.

Content:
Thermal threshold hopping & fairshare probe — incident notes and runbook sketch

Summary:
This doc captures the post-incident capture and an early runbook sketch for a class of degradation we saw on Dedicated GPU pools where the hosts briefly \"therm-hop\" (thermal sensor hysteresis causes repeated clock/frequency scaling) while the fairshare allocator simultaneously reshuffles slots, producing amplified latency and sporadic request failures. Felt different from classic noisy-tenant eviction: root appears to be short thermal excursions + aggressive fairshare rebalancing during the same time window.

Why this matters:
- Customers observed token-level latency spikes (p50 -> p99) and a handful of generation failures when their workloads got rescheduled mid-KV-cache warmup.
- Autoscaler reacted to apparent throughput drops and started provisioning/evicting, which made the oscillation worse.
- Short-lived (1–8 minutes) but high-impact: user-visible errors and SLO alert...

Content:
Alignment-induced waste & page-compaction simulation — notes

Summary:

Observed a class of OOMs that correlate poorly with total resident usage reported by nvidia-smi but correlate strongly with allocation failure patterns in logs. Hypothesis: alignment and page-granularity placement inside our slab/mempool allocator causes internal fragmentation that prevents reuse co-location; under certain allocation size mixes the allocator leaves small unusable gaps that cannot be satisfied by cudamalloc/cudamallocAsync fallback paths.

This doc captures experiments, a lightweight simulator we ran to quantify alignment waste, telemetry queries to find likely customers/clusters, and short-term mitigations to reduce the outage surface. The goal is to offer reproducible steps and concrete, low-risk mitigations we can push in the next minor runtime release.

Key observations from field incidents:
- OOM manifests as a sudden cudaMalloc retry loop with repeated ...

Client-side tokenization offload and degraded-mode strategy

Purpose and high-level motivation

This note captures a proposal for performing optional client-side tokenization and an engineered degraded-mode when client tokenization is not available or fails. The goal is to reduce p99 end-to-end latency and pressure on server-side tokenizer pools for short chat requests, while preserving correct KV cache behavior and consistency for longer contexts and reruns. It also outlines how this offload interacts with KV cache semantics, kernel scheduling, and batching heuristics in the serving runtime.

Why we need this

Observed pattern: many latency-sensitive calls are small prompt completions or chat turns where the server tokenizer contributes a significant fraction of the absolute request latency, especially at p95/p99 when tokenizer pools are congested. Summary:
This doc captures the post-incident capture and an early runbook sketch for a class of degradation we saw on Dedicated GPU pools where the hosts briefly \"therm-hop\" (thermal sensor hysteresis causes repeated clock/frequency scaling) while the fairshare allocator simultaneously reshuffles slots, producing amplified latency and sporadic request failures. Content:
Alignment-induced waste & page-compaction simulation — notes

Summary:

Observed a class of OOMs that correlate poorly with total resident usage reported by nvidia-smi but correlate strongly with allocation failure patterns in logs. Hypothesis: alignment and page-granularity placement inside our slab/mempool allocator causes internal fragmentation that prevents reuse co-location; under certain allocation size mixes the allocator leaves small unusable gaps that cannot be satisfied by cudamalloc/cudamallocAsync fallback paths.

This doc captures experiments, a lightweight simulator we ran to quantify alignment waste, telemetry queries to find likely customers/clusters, and short-term mitigations to reduce the outage surface. The goal is to offer reproducible steps and concrete, low-risk mitigations we can push in the next minor runtime release.

Key observations from field incidents:
- OOM manifests as a sudden cudaMalloc retry loop with repeated ...

The system should be observable, configurable per-customer and per-route, and support canary rollout.

Source event id: activity-eng-999420-07f6a3c4c573
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-999420
Actor: rajan-patel
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Regional Preference Horizon: latency-first failover + capacity-aware pinning

Problem: We see occasional cross-region flaps where clients are bounced between regions during short-lived spikes. That increases tail latency and cache churn and occasionally violates our 99p latency SLOs for some customers. Goal: implement a 'Regional Preference Horizon' policy that prefers low-latency regions but pins a requesting principal to the chosen region for a short horizon, and that gracefully fails over when capacity or latency deteriorates. Key constraints: preserve KV cache locality where possible, avoid long pins that hamper load balancing, integrate with existing circuit-breaker signals and token-shedder, and keep policy deterministic for debugging. This ticket designs and implements the scoring, pin/horizon semantics, and capacity-aware fallback behavior. It should also include experiments to validate SLO improvement and cost impact.

---

Source event id: activity-eng-218210-b175bd95cbcd
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-218210
Actor: marcus-patel
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
KV prefetch stream-lag causing slab orphaning and slow resident growth

Summary: Under sustained continuous-batching workloads with aggressive KV prefetching, we observe slow, persistent GPU resident-memory growth that does not plateau. Growth appears as small, scattered allocations that never get coalesced back into arena free lists (we're calling this 'slab orphaning'). Hypothesis: KV prefetch tasks allocate pinned/staging buffers and small device buffers across many streams; due to stream-lag on frees (deferred-free until stream completion) plus cudaMallocAsync pooling behavior we keep creating micro-slab fragments that the pool never reclaims. This is distinct from a single large leak — it's a slow slope caused by accumulation of tiny allocations that escape compaction heuristics.

Content:
Autotune sieve for cache keys and throttled TTL apply

Purpose: Build an automated, safety-first pipeline that suggests cache-key normalization (key shaping), per-prefix TTL recommendations, and applies changes using a throttled, reversible rollout (sieve) to minimize cost while bounding user-facing risk. The system should produce: 1) per-prefix hit-rate forecasts and uncertainty bounds, 2) recommended TTL deltas and key-normalization rules ranked by expected cost savings and risk, and 3) an automated throttled-applier that stages changes with canary cohorts, shadow replays, and automatic rollback if quality/cost deltas violate safety gates.

Background: We have a history of manual TTL tuning and ad-hoc key adjustments leading to either missed savings or regressions. Prior artifacts explored hit-modeling and TTL autoadaptation but lacked a single safe apply mechanism that combines key normalization + TTL changes in one controlled pipeline...

Actor: marco-rivera
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
cross-region-canary-mismatch-safe-promotion-gate-fix

During a staged model rollout spanning eu-west and us-east regions, the control plane recorded a canary state drift: the promotion decision computed in the primary region did not sync to the secondary region's route table. This caused ~12% of traffic to be routed to an older pinned model in us-east while eu-west moved forward. The mismatch triggered an automated rollback attempt in us-east that failed due to version-pinlock logic, creating a transient dual-serve state and elevated tail latency for several customer routes. This ticket captures the post-incident follow-ups, proposed fixes, and ownership for preventing cross-region canary divergence.

Goal: Create a coordinated initiative to harmonize batching tiers, KV prefix caching policies, and quantization profiles across high-volume customer routes to achieve a sustainable 18-25% unit-cost reduction without degrading core quality SLAs. This ticket tracks milestones, experiment matrix, rollout gates, and post-rollout monitoring/contracts.

Content:
Hybrid stochastic router with kernel-aware prefill probes and rollback playbook

Goal: Implement a hybrid stochastic routing layer that uses a fast prefill probe to compute a calibrated acceptance score combining predicted decode latency, kernel-fingerprint mismatch penalty, and decode-confidence (probe entropy). If the acceptance score exceeds a tunable threshold the request proceeds on an optimistic fast-path using a lighter-weight model/kernel variant; otherwise it falls back to the conservative full-path. The router must be kernel-selection-aware: model variants are annotated with a kernel fingerprint and estimated per-step cost so the acceptance function penalizes kernel mismatch or non-quantized execution paths.

Content:
Dynamic memory cadence for shared GPU safety

Content:
Continuous flow optimizer and kernel negotiator

Context: Persistent tail latency spikes on multi-tenant prefills despite kernel-level heuristics. The system should be observable, configurable per-customer and per-route, and support canary rollout.

Implement a configurable control set for Private/VPC/on-prem customers to: 1) enforce regional data residency boundaries for storage (logs, KV caches, artifacts) and model artifacts, 2) provide retention policy configuration and automated deletion evidence, and 3) define regional failover constraints that prevent cross-region data movement except under operator-authorized scenarios. Content:
Repair spike-induced silent period for roofline metrics and span handoff gaps affecting customer dashboards

Summary: During a sustained cross-tenant ingest surge on 2026-03-07 we observed a 7-12 minute silent window where roofline (per-tenant token/latency) metrics dropped to zero and tracing spans failed to stitch for affected tenants. This resulted in blank live panels for a subset of Hosted and Dedicated customers and escalations from two enterprise customers. Scope: Hosted API, Dedicated GPU pools, and Private deployments using the console viewer. Goal: implement a robust fix that prevents short-term ingest bursts from causing metric eviction + span handoff failures and add mitigations to surface missing signals faster in the console.

Source event id: activity-eng-999420-07f6a3c4c573

Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Regional Preference Horizon: latency-first failover + capacity-aware pinning

Problem: We see occasional cross-region flaps where clients are bounced between regions during short-lived spikes. That increases tail latency and cache churn and occasionally violates our 99p latency SLOs for some customers. Goal: implement a 'Regional Preference Horizon' policy that prefers low-latency regions but pins a requesting principal to the chosen region for a short horizon, and that gracefully fails over when capacity or latency deteriorates. Key constraints: preserve KV cache locality where possible, avoid long pins that hamper load balancing, integrate with existing circuit-breaker signals and token-shedder, and keep policy deterministic for debugging. This ticket designs and implements the scoring, pin/horizon semantics, and capacity-aware fallback behavior. It should also include experiments to validate SLO improvement and cost impact.

Source event id: activity-eng-218210-b175bd95cbcd
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-218210
Actor: marcus-patel
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
KV prefetch stream-lag causing slab orphaning and slow resident growth

Summary: Under sustained continuous-batching workloads with aggressive KV prefetching, we observe slow, persistent GPU resident-memory growth that does not plateau. Growth appears as small, scattered allocations that never get coalesced back into arena free lists (we're calling this 'slab orphaning'). Hypothesis: KV prefetch tasks allocate pinned/staging buffers and small device buffers across many streams; due to stream-lag on frees (deferred-free until stream completion) plus cudaMallocAsync pooling behavior we keep creating micro-slab fragments that the pool never reclaims. This is distinct from a single large leak — it's a slow slope caused by accumulation of tiny allocations that escape compaction heuristics.

Content:
Autotune sieve for cache keys and throttled TTL apply

Purpose: Build an automated, safety-first pipeline that suggests cache-key normalization (key shaping), per-prefix TTL recommendations, and applies changes using a throttled, reversible rollout (sieve) to minimize cost while bounding user-facing risk. The system should produce: 1) per-prefix hit-rate forecasts and uncertainty bounds, 2) recommended TTL deltas and key-normalization rules ranked by expected cost savings and risk, and 3) an automated throttled-applier that stages changes with canary cohorts, shadow replays, and automatic rollback if quality/cost deltas violate safety gates.

Background: We have a history of manual TTL tuning and ad-hoc key adjustments leading to either missed savings or regressions. Prior artifacts explored hit-modeling and TTL autoadaptation but lacked a single safe apply mechanism that combines key normalization + TTL changes in one controlled pipeline...

Content:
cross-region-canary-mismatch-safe-promotion-gate-fix

During a staged model rollout spanning eu-west and us-east regions, the control plane recorded a canary state drift: the promotion decision computed in the primary region did not sync to the secondary region's route table. This caused ~12% of traffic to be routed to an older pinned model in us-east while eu-west moved forward. The mismatch triggered an automated rollback attempt in us-east that failed due to version-pinlock logic, creating a transient dual-serve state and elevated tail latency for several customer routes. This ticket captures the post-incident follow-ups, proposed fixes, and ownership for preventing cross-region canary divergence.

Usage-windowing credits pilot for observability, compliance pack, and premium SLA

Goal: Validate a usage-windowing credit model that lets enterprise customers allocate discrete observability credits (retention/day, query credits) and optionally bundle a Compliance Pack + Premium SLA. Hypothesis: Offering time-bounded credits and a small, clearly-priced Compliance Pack will increase attach rate for paid observability tiers by 12% and raise average contract value for mid-market customers. Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Deployment circuit-breaker and customer escalation runway

Problem: Recent model rollouts have produced intermittent semantic regressions in hosted customers and divergent behavior between Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments due to differing kernel selection and KV cache priming. The absence of a fast, unilateral stop mechanism that can be applied consistently across environments delays mitigation and complicates customer communications.

Goal: Implement a deployment 'circuit-breaker' service and accompanying operational runway (alerts, playbooks, customer comms templates) that allows SRE/Eng to safely stop a rollout (per-model, per-region, per-customer-tier), triage the regression, and either rollback or hotpatch within a documented SLA window. The solution must work across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments and preserve data residency and tenant isolation constraints.

Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Tenant-ratecap adaptive phase controller for latency-fair throughput

Develop an adaptive per-tenant phase controller that dynamically adjusts prefill/decode phase split and tenant rate caps to meet tenant SLOs while maximizing cluster throughput and kernel utilization. The controller should combine latency SLO feedback, token budget windows, and signals from kernel selection and attention-sparsity probes to decide: (1) whether to split a request into finer-grained phases, (2) whether to throttle or prioritize a tenant in the current timeslice, and (3) when to prefer lower-cost kernel paths or quantized model variants for opportunistic throughput. This ticket covers design, simulation, and a safe canary rollout plan.

This ticket adds a lightweight runtime probing layer (scout) that issues microbench probes and maintains a per-op-path confidence score to pick safe, cost-effective kernels for prefill and decode phases separately.

Content:
Shadow traffic broker with tenant slab accounting and proportional sampling

Goal: implement a production-ready shadow traffic broker that safely duplicates a subset of live requests to candidate model variants while enforcing per-tenant cost slabs, strong isolation, and automatic rollback triggers. This ticket designs and implements: 1) a broker service (stateless control plane + per-node state) that maintains tenant slabs and admission logic; 2) a proportional/stratified sampling engine that adapts sampling probability based on each tenant's remaining slab and observed budget burn rate; 3) a sandboxed execution path for mirrored requests (sidecar isolation + non-producing metrics) to avoid customer-facing regressions; 4) integration points with the rollout controller for auto-rollback when shadow-driven metrics regress beyond thresholds.

Actor: ava-chen
Occurred at: 1773057600000
Content:
weave-attn: chunked fused-attention kernel + windowed KV caching + continuous-batcher

Context and motivation:
We see two recurring pain points in production: (1) long-context generation workloads suffer from high memory pressure and degraded throughput due to monolithic KV cache handling, and (2) mixed small/large request workloads cause suboptimal batching decisions that harm latency-sensitive routes. This PR introduces a combined runtime change targeted at those two areas: a fused, chunked attention kernel ("weave-attn") that processes attention in contiguous chunks to improve memory locality and vectorization, plus a windowed KV caching layer that shards and trims KV state by logical token windows, and a continuous-batcher micro-scheduler that keeps the GPU feeding while preserving latency SLOs for small requests.

Actor: aisha-rahman
Occurred at: 1773057600000
Content:
Shadow-tiling cohort convergence + rollback-oracle integration

Motivation: As we run more shadow traffic experiments at scale, individual cohorts that overlap (by user, session or feature flag) create noisy signals that delay safe promotions. This PR introduces 'shadow-tiling' — a deterministic tiling scheme that partitions shadow traffic into cohort-convergent slices and wires a rollback-oracle that recommends automated short-circuit rollbacks if multi-signal divergence is detected.

What this PR does: - Introduces a server-side deterministic tiling algorithm which maps incoming requests into shadow tiles based on a convergent hash (combining user_id, country, and experiment_salt). Tiles are stable under resharding and respect session affinity. - Adds a cohort convergence evaluation step to the rollout controller: each tile reports metric deltas (latency, error rate, quality regressions), and the controller aggregates with outlier-resistant stat...

Actor: maya-singh
Occurred at: 1773057600000
Content:
Invocation Sankey visualization and proportional alert-smoothing for long-tail token noise

Motivation: Our token-level telemetry surfaced lots of useful per-phase metrics but customers struggle to connect which token phases (prefix, generation, kv-restore, post-processing) are responsible for cost/latency spikes. Separately, alert churn for token-cost SLOs remains high because small invocations with large variance trigger the same thresholds as high-volume endpoints. This PR introduces two complementary observability features: 1) a Sankey-style aggregation (invocation -> token-phase -> cost/latency bucket) to make per-invocation cost flow explorable, and 2) a proportional alert-smoothing algorithm that weights threshold signals by invocation mass to reduce flapping on long-tail noise.

What I changed: single-string summary of changes across repos and services.

Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1773057600000
Content:
Handshake escrow and client-abort semantics for streaming sessions (OpenAI compatibility)

Motivation: intermittent client disconnects during long-running streaming responses cause two classes of problems: 1) clients that reconnect within a short window are unable to resume because the server immediately tears down session state; 2) client-initiated cancels (or network-triggered resets) have ambiguous semantics with respect to tool/function call idempotency and structured output flushing. This PR introduces a handshake escrow token and clarified client-abort semantics to make resume/reconnect and abort behavior deterministic and OpenAI-compatible. Key changes: - Introduce a short-lived session escrow ticket (Escrow-Token) retained server-side for a configurable grace window to support reconnect-and-resume for active stream sessions. The token is returned in initial stream response headers and via the OpenAI-compat protocol extension. - Define deterministic semantics for cli...

Actor: ava-chen
Occurred at: 1773057600000
Content:
weave-attn: chunked fused-attention kernel + windowed KV caching + continuous-batcher

Context and motivation:
We see two recurring pain points in production: (1) long-context generation workloads suffer from high memory pressure and degraded throughput due to monolithic KV cache handling, and (2) mixed small/large request workloads cause suboptimal batching decisions that harm latency-sensitive routes. This PR introduces a combined runtime change targeted at those two areas: a fused, chunked attention kernel ("weave-attn") that processes attention in contiguous chunks to improve memory locality and vectorization, plus a windowed KV caching layer that shards and trims KV state by logical token windows, and a continuous-batcher micro-scheduler that keeps the GPU feeding while preserving latency SLOs for small requests.

Design summary (high level):
- Fused chunked-attention kernel (runtime/attention/weave_kernel.*): implements a tiled attention path that accepts a per-re...

Content:
Shadow-tiling cohort convergence + rollback-oracle integration

Motivation: As we run more shadow traffic experiments at scale, individual cohorts that overlap (by user, session or feature flag) create noisy signals that delay safe promotions. This PR introduces 'shadow-tiling' — a deterministic tiling scheme that partitions shadow traffic into cohort-convergent slices and wires a rollback-oracle that recommends automated short-circuit rollbacks if multi-signal divergence is detected.

What this PR does: - Introduces a server-side deterministic tiling algorithm which maps incoming requests into shadow tiles based on a convergent hash (combining user_id, country, and experiment_salt). Tiles are stable under resharding and respect session affinity. - Adds a cohort convergence evaluation step to the rollout controller: each tile reports metric deltas (latency, error rate, quality regressions), and the controller aggregates with outlier-resistant stat...

Actor: maya-singh

Content:
Invocation Sankey visualization and proportional alert-smoothing for long-tail token noise

Motivation: Our token-level telemetry surfaced lots of useful per-phase metrics but customers struggle to connect which token phases (prefix, generation, kv-restore, post-processing) are responsible for cost/latency spikes. Separately, alert churn for token-cost SLOs remains high because small invocations with large variance trigger the same thresholds as high-volume endpoints. This PR introduces two complementary observability features: 1) a Sankey-style aggregation (invocation -> token-phase -> cost/latency bucket) to make per-invocation cost flow explorable, and 2) a proportional alert-smoothing algorithm that weights threshold signals by invocation mass to reduce flapping on long-tail noise.

What I changed: single-string summary of changes across repos and services.
- Telemetry ingestion: add token_phase_flow metric (histogram + edges) and sankey_edge_{cost,latency} tags emitted ...

Source URL: /github?repo=redwood&tab=pulls&pr=91763
Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1773057600000
Content:
Handshake escrow and client-abort semantics for streaming sessions (OpenAI compatibility)

Motivation: intermittent client disconnects during long-running streaming responses cause two classes of problems: 1) clients that reconnect within a short window are unable to resume because the server immediately tears down session state; 2) client-initiated cancels (or network-triggered resets) have ambiguous semantics with respect to tool/function call idempotency and structured output flushing. This PR introduces a handshake escrow token and clarified client-abort semantics to make resume/reconnect and abort behavior deterministic and OpenAI-compatible. Key changes: - Introduce a short-lived session escrow ticket (Escrow-Token) retained server-side for a configurable grace window to support reconnect-and-resume for active stream sessions. The token is returned in initial stream response headers and via the OpenAI-compat protocol extension. - Define deterministic semantics for cli...

Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Latent signal filtering and gradual region evacuation playbook

Problem: We continue to see noisy paging in regional SLO alerts caused by transient spikes and correlated downstream timeouts. On-call teams often escalate to full region failover too quickly, causing unnecessary traffic shifts and cache churn.

Goals:
- Reduce noisy pages by 40% for regional latency/ErrorRate SLOs without increasing time-to-detect true incidents.
- Implement a graduated region evacuation path that can be triggered automatically or manually, with clear circuit-breaker thresholds.
- Ship a deterministic runbook and automation hooks so runbooks can be executed partially (soft-failover) or fully (evacuation) and be reversed safely.

Scope:
In scope: alert tuning for regional SLOs, regional circuit-breaker design, staged load-shedding (gradual evacuation), on-call runbook authoring, and automation integration (PagerDuty / Slack / Runbook Automation).
Out of scope: global ...

Actor: marcus-lee
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Optimistic late-bind decode planner: kernel-aware model routing, probe acceptance metrics, and staged fallback

Build an optimistic, late-bind decode planning layer that can route requests to the fastest compatible model/kernel variant by using a lightweight preexecution probe and an acceptance metric. Provide a staged fallback to more conservative decoders when the acceptance signal indicates low confidence or kernel mismatch. Goal: reduce tail latency for common short generation workloads while preserving correctness and predictable degrade behavior.

Source event id: activity-eng-524789-db3676174d9c
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-524789
Actor: diego-martinez
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Route header canonicalization bug led to cross-variant serve and stalled rollback

Summary: During a staged canary promotion of model v3.1 -> v3.2 we observed a routing mismatch caused by header canonicalization in the edge translator. Requests intended for the canary split (10%) were partially misrouted to the baseline variant due to duplicate header normalization (X-Route and x-route), which resulted in inconsistent KV cache keys and mixed-serve responses. Attempts to rollback were blocked by a pinned-version latch on several router instances, causing a ~42 minute period of high error variance and increased tail latency for affected customers. This ticket captures timeline, root cause analysis, mitigation, and action items.

recall.text:
Symptoms: steady increase in resident GPU memory across the lifetime of a process, apparent free space that isn't being re-used (lots of small holes), and cudaMallocAsync fallbacks/retries that correlate with request latency bumps. Not a single catastrophic allocation — rather fragmented free space prevents large contiguous allocations (workspace/extents) even though aggregate free memory is sufficient.

Goal: build a lightweight triage checklist + a few reproducible experiments and quick mitigation ideas so on-call and runtime engineers can quickly scope and apply fixes. Long-term: changes to the mempool coalescence heuristics and better telemetry for hole sizes + allocation histograms.

Goal: build a lightweight triage checklist + a few reproducible experiments and quick mitigation ideas so on-call and runtime engineers can quickly scope and apply fixes. Long-term: changes to the mempool coalescence heuristics and better telemetry for hole sizes + allocation histograms.

Content:
Geo-redundant fleet placement, throughput SLAs and fleet governance

Define product requirements, policy model, and rollout plan for geo-redundant Dedicated fleets that support: (1) placement constraints (region-only, multi-zone, zone-preferred), (2) throughput guarantees (per-tenant and per-route token/sec SLAs), and (3) controlled failover behavior subject to data residency constraints. This ticket scopes the PM-level acceptance criteria, API surface for placement & failover policy, billing implications for throughput SLAs, and coordination needs with Engineering and Compliance for auditability.

Source event id: activity-eng-50621-92f0bffd3914
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-50621
Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1740830400000
Content:
Predictive-reserve autoscale + controlled soft-shrink for multi-zone dedicated pools under residency constraints

Problem: Customers running multi-zone dedicated fleets need predictable throughput and non-disruptive capacity changes while also satisfying strict regional/data-residency constraints. Current autoscaler can add capacity reactively but: (a) often overshoots under bursty workloads causing cost spikes, and (b) tears down capacity immediately on scale-in which can violate soft-SLOs for request latency. We also lack a clear mechanism to ensure residency locks during aggressive autoscale operations across zones.

Proposal: Implement a predictive-reserve autoscaling mode that allocates a small, time-windowed reserve based on short-term forecasts and recent tail-latency signals, paired with a configurable "soft-shrink" workflow for scale-in that drains capacity gradually (evicting new allocations first, migrating existing KV cache prefixes off the node, and reducing offered slots in steps). ...

Source URL: /linear?team=product-management&view=list&issue=PM-51802

Content:
Incremental canary + backfill playbook for verified model signatures and pinning

Create an operational playbook and automated tooling to safely canary new Redwood-verified performance signatures and backfill performance profiles for previously onboarded model versions. The playbook will define telemetry gates, automated backfill jobs, pin semantics, and rollback criteria so that engineering and ops teams can publish verified profiles with minimal manual coordination.

Source event id: activity-eng-531247-3d40f213ea7c
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-531247
Actor: diego-alvarez
Occurred at: 1740830400000
Content:
Length-tier prefetch coordinator to stabilize continuous batching for mixed-length dialogs

Problem: Mixed prompt/response workloads (chat apps with long system prompts + very short user turns, support dialogs with long context and short responses) cause three operational issues for the continuous batcher: 1) KV cache fragmentation and high residency for a small fraction of sessions, 2) inefficient slot packing where long-turns block batch progress and increase tail latency, and 3) GPU memory spike events during lane demotion or cold prefetch that trigger OOMs or aggressive eviction policies.

Goal: Implement a length-tier prefetch coordinator that layers admission control, predictive prefetching and memory-aware slot assignment to reduce tail latency and KV pressure while preserving throughput. Targets: reduce 95th-percentile generation latency by ~20% on mixed workloads, reduce KV cache churn by 15-25%, and eliminate OOM spikes from mixed-length bursts in canary clusters.
...

Source event id: activity-pm-65789-88527a7d35e6
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=product-management&view=list&issue=PM-65789
Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1740830400000
Content:
Harmonized Intent Surface PRD + Launch Acceptance Checklist

Problem: Customers maintain multiple integration paths for chat, completions, embeddings, and rerank, increasing SDK complexity and causing inconsistent behavior across features. Goal: Ship a harmonized, intent-driven Hosted API surface that lets a single endpoint accept an explicit 'intent' field and optional hints, and routes requests to the optimal serving path (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) with deterministic fallback and transparent billing. This ticket defines the PRD, success criteria, and launch checklist for the minimum-safe launch of the Harmonized Intent Surface.

Quarterly roadmap slice that defines how we will align dedicated capacity reservation (commitments) with autoscaling policies and rollout gates. This ticket captures the end-to-end plan for Q2 focused work: (1) a hedging strategy for reservation sizing to reduce overcommit and underprovision risk, (2) a set of autoscale primitives and policy templates for predictable throughput, and (3) measurable OKRs tying SLOs and unit economics to product milestones. The document intentionally maps work into sprint-sized deliveries, articulation of acceptance criteria, decision trade-offs (cost vs latency vs reliability), and dashboards we need to ship before canary rollouts to enterprise customers.

Source event id: activity-eng-231456-59f4ee4861f9
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-231456
Actor: mateo-alvarez
Occurred at: 1740830400000
Content:
Allocation throttler and reuse-hinting API for multi-tenant paged-attention

Problem: multi-tenant workloads with bursty long-context requests cause disproportionate KV residency pressure and spike decode tail latency for other tenants. Existing LRU-ish reclamation reacts too late and kernel candidacy selection suffers when prefills are evicted mid-decode.

Goal: implement a lightweight allocation throttler that enforces soft per-tenant allocation budgets for paged KV while exposing a reuse-hinting API so higher-level schedulers (routing, tenant-weighting, canary controllers) can mark fragments/pages as high-reuse or ephemeral. This should reduce cross-tenant thrashing, keep decode tail SLOs stable, and improve kernel selection decisions by providing residency stability signals.

Scope: design and implement runtime-side throttler + public/internal reuse-hinting API + admission rules in the prefill pipeline. Instrument and measure impact on tail latency, evicti...

---

Source event id: activity-eng-160999-dafe6b30f150
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-160999
Actor: marco-rivera
Occurred at: 1740830400000
Content:
Interruptible generation garbage collection and client resume protocol

Background: Partial token generations may accumulate when clients disconnect mid-stream (network failures, client crashes, or long-poll timeouts). Left unmanaged these partials consume memory and KV cache slots and complicate function/tool-call atomicity. This ticket designs and implements a low-latency GC and resume protocol that: 1) ensures quick reclamation of resources from abandoned generations, 2) preserves ability for well-behaved clients to resume recent partial outputs without re-generation, and 3) maintains strong invariants for structured outputs and tool/function calling so downstream side-effects are not duplicated.

Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-207333
Actor: diego-morales
Occurred at: 1740830400000
Content:
Microeconomist: continuous batching recommendation pipeline (SLO-aware heuristics & safe apply)

Goal: Build a continuous recommendation engine (Microeconomist) that synthesizes batching and caching configuration suggestions per workload while guaranteeing SLO-safe applies and automated rollback. The system uses lightweight per-workload microeconometric models to trade latency, cost, and token-efficiency signals and emits ranked recommended parameter sets (microbatch size, kv-window, prefix-caching TTL, model-variant fallback rules).

Scope:
- Telemetry + profiler: collect per-route sequence length distribution, cold/hot KV cache hit rates, token latency P50/P95, and per-token cost estimates over sliding windows.
- Heuristics & scoring: implement SLO-aware scoring that penalizes recommendations likely to violate latency SLOs, and prioritize configs that reduce estimated token cost by >3% with high confidence.
- Continuous apply: propose a shadow-apply -> canary -> gradual rollout...

Content:
Add exemplar shedder sidecar to limit hotpath tracing costs

Problem: Ingress and application-side tracing hooks remain a significant CPU and allocation hotspot in high-QPS services. Current tail-based and adaptive sampling improvements reduce trace volume but still require encoding spans and allocating buffers on the hot path for many requests that end up unsampled. That per-request overhead inflates 95/99th latency and increases cost for Dedicated clusters.

Priority leakage causing execution-path starvation during capacity surge — action items and mitigations

What happened: Around 2026-02-18 03:14 UTC we observed a spike in p95/p99 latency across several models served on shared pools. Initial triage showed normal GPU utilization but elevated request queue times. Investigation traced the root cause to a scheduler policy mismatch: when scheduler attempted to reassign slots during a burst, the per-request priority bitset failed to clear correctly for certain preempted sessions. Concomitant KV-cache eviction hysteresis exacerbated token-level recomputation and cache-warmup delays. Actor: marco-patel
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Align session ephemerality with scheduler fairness and reduce KV memory churn

Problem: the serving runtime currently treats session objects and their KV cache entries as long-lived by default. This causes memory churn and uneven preemption behavior when the scheduler rebalances or preempts slots. Spike findings show frequent short-lived sessions (chat turn micro-sessions) allocate KV segments that survive GC windows, leading to tail memory pressure and SLO spikes. Goal: make session lifetime semantics explicit and align KV ephemerality to scheduler fairness so short sessions don't monopolize cache or cause prolonged eviction storms.

Content:
Postmortem: HPA/KEDA autoscaling regression causing false positives and cold-start spikes

Summary:
During a traffic surge on 2026-02-17 we observed repeated capacity events where the autoscaling layer (HPA + KEDA) incorrectly scaled down critical inference pods, then triggered rapid scale-ups producing a wave of cold-starts. This produced elevated tail latency for streaming and chat routes and increased token-cost by ~18% for affected customers.

What happened (high level):
- At 09:15 UTC an upstream burst of short-lived requests (many <100ms) increased request-per-pod variance.
- KEDA's external scaler metric pipeline filtered noisy signals incorrectly after a recent change to the metric aggregation window (PR: infra/metrics#719).
- HPA interpreted the absence of sustained high CPU as opportunity to scale down. Combined with fast scale-down TTL we observed pods terminate while KV caches were still warming.
- Subsequent scale-ups hit cold-start time and warming overhead; a...

Content:
Slate tile packer: adaptive tile-based batch formation with KV-tiling and GPU budget enforcement for streaming chat

Problem: For streaming chat completions we need a batch formation strategy that minimizes tail latency while maximizing GPU utilization and respecting a strict per-GPU memory budget. Existing continuous-batching approaches either over-consolidate multi-turn sessions (increasing KV cache pressure) or under-bundle short-turn traffic (wasting kernel throughput). This ticket proposes a new 'slate tile packer' heuristic that treats incoming streaming requests as tiles placed into a bounded slate per worker, with explicit KV-tiling and GPU memory budgeting to avoid OOMs and excessive KV thrashing.

KV cache eviction regression & poison-susceptibility follow-up

This ticket tracks follow-ups and action items from the postmortem aiming to fully remediate the regression, add guardrails, and close monitoring gaps.

Prefix registry with on-GPU redirect table and refcounted dedupe

Design and implement a compact on-GPU prefix registry that: 1) deduplicates identical prompt prefixes across concurrent and sequential requests via a content-addressed fingerprint, 2) uses a tiny on-GPU redirect table to map alias fingerprints to canonical KV locations, and 3) maintains lightweight reference counts to enable safe eviction and reclamation without frequent host roundtrips. Schema evolution and rehydration for compressed structured logs with correlation expansion

Content:
Phantom capacity from delayed decommission signals triggered fallback amplification

Summary: A backlog in the decommission signal pipeline produced stale 'available capacity' state for a decommissioning region. Traffic continued to be routed there for a short window, creating hot shards. Hot shard rebalancing then pushed load into fallback variants/tiers and cross-region routes, amplifying latency and causing an elevated rate of model-variant fallbacks across multiple customers. This ticket documents the incident, mitigations, and follow-ups.

Content:
Microburst stall absorber and adaptive admission policy

Problem: We observe brief microburst traffic patterns that cause single-digit-millisecond spikes at p99+ for prefill->decode handoffs. Root cause analysis shows: (1) sudden admission of long-prefill requests overwhelms kernel slots; (2) attention-cache prewarm misses create decode stalls; (3) current kernel selector is slow to pick a lower-latency kernel for short sequences, causing head-of-line latency amplification.

Goal: Implement a runtime component (Microburst Stall Absorber) that combines an adaptive admission policy, micro-sliced batch shaping, and fast kernel-fallback hints to reduce p99/p999 latencies during microbursts without materially reducing aggregate throughput. Target: reduce p99 by >=25% and p999 by >=30% for mixed traffic traces from customer workloads while keeping throughput loss <=5%.

- Admission: add a microburst detector based on moving-windo...

This produced elevated tail latency for streaming and chat routes and increased token-cost by ~18% for affected customers.

What happened (high level):
- At 09:15 UTC an upstream burst of short-lived requests (many <100ms) increased request-per-pod variance.
- KEDA's external scaler metric pipeline filtered noisy signals incorrectly after a recent change to the metric aggregation window (PR: infra/metrics#719).
- HPA interpreted the absence of sustained high CPU as opportunity to scale down. Combined with fast scale-down TTL we observed pods terminate while KV caches were still warming.
- Subsequent scale-ups hit cold-start time and warming overhead; a...

Actor: mateo-alvarez
Occurred at: 1773316800000
Content:
Slate tile packer: adaptive tile-based batch formation with KV-tiling and GPU budget enforcement for streaming chat

Problem: For streaming chat completions we need a batch formation strategy that minimizes tail latency while maximizing GPU utilization and respecting a strict per-GPU memory budget. Existing continuous-batching approaches either over-consolidate multi-turn sessions (increasing KV cache pressure) or under-bundle short-turn traffic (wasting kernel throughput). This ticket proposes a new 'slate tile packer' heuristic that treats incoming streaming requests as tiles placed into a bounded slate per worker, with explicit KV-tiling and GPU memory budgeting to avoid OOMs and excessive KV thrashing.

Content:
IAM Guardrails Terraform module for Private VPC deployments

Create a reusable Terraform module that provides opinionated IAM/RBAC guardrails for Redwood Private (VPC) deployments. This module should enable safe, least-privilege defaults for Customer VPC onboarding, provide easy cross-account role templates for control-plane <> tenant account relationships, and include upgrade/rollout paths and health checks suitable for CI/CD pipelines and automated audits. The goal is to reduce onboarding friction, avoid over-privileged bootstrap roles, and make upgrades (policy changes or role rotations) auditable and reversible.

KV cache eviction regression & poison-susceptibility follow-up

Summary: During a production capacity run on 2026-02-28 we observed repeated latency spikes and degraded throughput across several hosted clusters. This ticket tracks follow-ups and action items from the postmortem aiming to fully remediate the regression, add guardrails, and close monitoring gaps.

Content:
Prefix registry with on-GPU redirect table and refcounted dedupe

Design and implement a compact on-GPU prefix registry that: 1) deduplicates identical prompt prefixes across concurrent and sequential requests via a content-addressed fingerprint, 2) uses a tiny on-GPU redirect table to map alias fingerprints to canonical KV locations, and 3) maintains lightweight reference counts to enable safe eviction and reclamation without frequent host roundtrips. This ticket covers design, prototype, integration with continuous batching, and a staged rollout plan. Goals: reduce duplicate KV allocations, lower peak GPU memory by avoiding duplicated prefix KV blobs, and preserve latency SLOs under bursty request arrival patterns.

Schema evolution and rehydration for compressed structured logs with correlation expansion

We are ingesting increasingly dense structured logs from edge sidecars using a lossless compression + compact schema header. Content:
Phantom capacity from delayed decommission signals triggered fallback amplification

Summary: A backlog in the decommission signal pipeline produced stale 'available capacity' state for a decommissioning region. Traffic continued to be routed there for a short window, creating hot shards. Hot shard rebalancing then pushed load into fallback variants/tiers and cross-region routes, amplifying latency and causing an elevated rate of model-variant fallbacks across multiple customers. This ticket documents the incident, mitigations, and follow-ups.

Content:
Microburst stall absorber and adaptive admission policy

Problem: We observe brief microburst traffic patterns that cause single-digit-millisecond spikes at p99+ for prefill->decode handoffs. Root cause analysis shows: (1) sudden admission of long-prefill requests overwhelms kernel slots; (2) attention-cache prewarm misses create decode stalls; (3) current kernel selector is slow to pick a lower-latency kernel for short sequences, causing head-of-line latency amplification.

Goal: Implement a runtime component (Microburst Stall Absorber) that combines an adaptive admission policy, micro-sliced batch shaping, and fast kernel-fallback hints to reduce p99/p999 latencies during microbursts without materially reducing aggregate throughput. Target: reduce p99 by >=25% and p999 by >=30% for mixed traffic traces from customer workloads while keeping throughput loss <=5%.

- Admission: add a microburst detector based on moving-windo...

recall.text:
team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-47216
Actor: jonah-park
Occurred at: 1773489600000
Content:
Progressive traffic steering and session-affinity reconciliation for active-active streaming handoff

Problem: Hosted API customers use long-lived streaming connections and sessionful chat flows. During region-level capacity pressure or partial outages we must shift traffic between regions without truncating in-flight streams, violating latency SLOs, or causing mass reconnect storms. Current approach (hard cutover + circuit-based fallback) results in abrupt stream terminations and an inconsistent session cache state that complicates resumption.

Goal: Implement a progressive steering and session-affinity reconciliation mechanism that lets the edge perform gradual traffic shifts while preserving stream continuity or providing a resumable handoff path with clear SLO behavior and bounded customer impact. The design must integrate edge-side circuit breakers and probabilistic load-shedding so we maintain p95 latency and error-rate SLOs during shifts and scale events.

---

Source event id: activity-eng-218765-ef38faf5a89e
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-218765
Actor: diego-mart-nez
Occurred at: 1773489600000
Content:
Speculative commit arbitration with kernel-cost curation and soft rollback playbook

Problem: We need a low-latency, high-availability speculative decode flow that minimizes wasted cycles on expensive kernels while preserving output quality. Current optimistic decode flows accept candidate outputs aggressively which can waste high-cost kernel time or produce poor-quality responses when confidence signals are noisy. This ticket drafts a commit arbitration mechanism that gates speculative commits with a lightweight confidence signal, uses a kernel-cost curation step to prefer cheaper accept candidates early, and defines a soft rollback/fallback playbook when late signals indicate the optimistic branch is invalid.

---

Source event id: activity-eng-77284-1c32b42e6fe0
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-77284
Actor: priya-natarajan
Occurred at: 1773489600000
Content:
Investigate HPA/KEDA sensitivity drift causing under-scaling during warm-start bursts

Summary: On 2026-03-10 we observed repeated under-scaling of model serving pools during multiple short warm-start bursts for the text-generation fleet in us-west-2. Symptoms were elevated 95th and 99th percentile latency spikes while overall CPU/GPU utilization remained well below autoscale thresholds. The effect manifested as degraded tail latency for a subset of customers (~2% of production traffic) for ~25 minutes.

What happened (brief): A change in KEDA scaling behavior combined with a drift in the HPA metric window exposed a gap where rapid bursts of new sessions (warm-starts that populate KV cache) caused transient token-generation latency to spike, but the scaling system did not react quickly enough due to the scaler's debounce/windowing configuration and a higher-than-expected metric noise floor. Our existing safeguards (minReplicas, fallback routing) did not fully prevent la...

Content:
Hybrid-reserved GPU fit & SLA triage playbook for Dedicated intake

Summary: Create an operational playbook and intake rubric to evaluate enterprise requests for Dedicated capacity that combine reserved throughput with limited autoscaling/burst (hybrid-reserved). This ticket defines the decision flow for SLA targets, reserved throughput sizing, GPU class selection, burst credit handling, and required preflight tests. Goal is to reduce time-to-offer for sales/solutions and ensure engineering can validate risk and operational cost before commitments.

Scope:
- Intake questionnaire (customer-facing) and internal scoring rubric (cost sensitivity, latency SLA, availability SLA, KV cache locality needs, model variants).
- GPU fit decision matrix that weighs model family, average sequence length, KV cache locality, and requested reserved throughput to recommend GPU class and quantization profile.

Content:
Privilege shadowing, SSO assertion hardening, and KMS audit sign-off

Clarify requirements, scope changes, and acceptance criteria for three tightly-coupled security controls for Private/VPC deployments: (1) privilege shadowing (temporary operator impersonation with auditable traces), (2) SSO/SAML assertion hardening and federation verification, and (3) KMS/HSM-integrated audit evidence and key-escrow operational controls. This ticket consolidates ambiguous PRD language, captures engineering constraints, and presents a sign-off checklist for security, legal, and product owners.

Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=product-management&view=list&issue=PM-100999
Actor: priya-natarajan
Occurred at: 1773144000000
Content:
Cross-Product Launch Calendar: Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator

Objective: Build a Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator (DRTO) that provides a single authoritative launch calendar and automated conflict-resolution layer for runtime, console, and API releases. DRTO should allow teams to reserve launch slots, attach canary windows, declare blackout windows and capacity reservations, and automatically compute safe stagger policies and fallback lanes when conflicts are detected.

Background: Launch friction has been a recurring source of latency and outages when multiple product teams schedule large runtime or console changes in overlapping windows. The current playbooks are manual (spreadsheets + Slack) and cause avoidable collisions, overbooked GPU capacity, and late-stage rollbacks. This initiative aims to reduce schedule conflicts, accelerate cross-team coordination, and provide automated signals for go/no-go decisions.

Scope (MVP):
- Central calen...

Source event id: activity-eng-275431-cecf67214b82
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-275431
Actor: marco-ruiz
Occurred at: 1773144000000
Content:
Model-variant fallback coordinator and customer communication pathway for mixed-deployment rollouts

When heterogeneous deployments (hosted, Dedicated, Private) receive staggered model updates we occasionally observe asymmetric regressions: a model variant passes synthetic and internal canaries but degrades on customer slices due to KV cache mismatch, tokenization edge cases, or hardware-specific kernel divergences. This ticket defines an automated fallback coordinator plus a customer-facing escalation pathway that: (1) detects tenant-scoped functional regressions quickly, (2) orchestrates variant fallback with KV cache reconciliation and traffic slicing, and (3) surfaces concise, actionable communications to affected customers and account teams.

---

Source event id: activity-eng-314159-66df29f32dfd
Source app: linear
Source URL: /linear?team=engineering&view=list&issue=ENG-314159
Actor: ravi-patel
Occurred at: 1773144000000
Content:
Delegated Shedding Policy with Grace Epochs and Per-Region Locality

Introduce a hybrid admission control pattern where global edge routers delegate fine-grained shedding decisions to lightweight per-region locality controllers. Each controller enforces a small set of policies (priority lanes, soft quotas, and grace epochs) that allow progressive fidelity degradation instead of hard drops. This ticket implements the policy, runtime hooks, and observability for rollout.

Introduce a hybrid admission control pattern where global edge routers delegate fine-grained shedding decisions to lightweight per-region locality controllers. Each controller enforces a small set of policies (priority lanes, soft quotas, and grace epochs) that allow progressive fidelity degradation instead of hard drops. This ticket implements the policy, runtime hooks, and observability for rollout.

question: What source records support the current RBAC and console-query direction?
answer:
**Current RBAC + Console‑query direction – source evidence**

| # | Source record (name / type) | Location / ID | What it proves / how it drives the direction |
|---|------------------------------|---------------|----------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **RBAC v2 Design Overview (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑design‑overview‑hosted‑dedicated‑private* | Defines the overall deny‑by‑default model, scope (org/project/env/cluster) and the fact that **all Console actions must be RBAC‑checked**. |
| 2 | **RBAC v2 + Least‑Privilege Enterprise FAQ** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑least‑privilege‑enterprise‑security‑faq* | Gives the “why” for least‑privilege enforcement and the “what” (permission catalog, audit‑log evidence) that the Console surface must expose. |
| 3 | **RBAC v2 Role Templates & Permission Matrix** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑role‑templates‑and‑permission‑matrix* | Canonical permission list that the Console UI uses to populate the role‑editor, permission‑search, and “why‑was‑I‑denied?” screens. |
| 4 | **RBAC v2 Permission Inventory & Classification (Canonical Catalog)** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑permission‑inventory‑classification* | The source of truth for every Console‑exposed action → permission mapping; drives the query that the Console performs to decide whether to enable a button or show an error. |
| 5 | **RBAC v2 Evaluation Flow Scratchpad** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑evaluation‑flow‑scratchpad* | Shows the end‑to‑end flow (gateway → control‑plane → Console) and proves that the Console must invoke the **central policy proxy** for each query. |
| 6 | **Console UX Spec: Role Management (RBAC v2)** | Confluence – *console‑ux‑spec‑role‑management‑rbac‑v2* | Detailed UI spec (role editor, template picker, permission viewer, “why denied?” panel) – the concrete artifact that implements the RBAC direction in the Console. |
| 7 | **RBAC v2 Rollout Runbook (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑rollout‑runbook‑hosted‑dedicated‑private* | Describes the staged enablement of RBAC v2, the feature‑flag flow, and the required Console changes (role‑binding UI, audit‑trail view). |
| 8 | **RBAC v2 Migration Risk Register** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑migration‑risk‑register* | Documents the risk‑assessment that forced the Console to add **RBAC‑aware query filters** (e.g., hide privileged actions from non‑admin users). |
| 9 | **Console Audit‑Trail View for Dedicated Rollout Actions** | Linear – *DES‑447* | UI design for an audit‑timeline that is filtered by the current user’s RBAC permissions – the first concrete “console‑query” artifact. |
|10| **PM‑661 – Console Role‑Management Scope Model** | Linear – *PM‑661* | Formal requirement that the Console must expose the four‑level scope hierarchy and that queries to the policy bundle respect that hierarchy. |
|11| **Central Policy Proxy & Immutable Audit Pipeline for RBAC v2** | Linear – *ENG‑10021* | Gives the engineering contract that the Console queries the **policy‑proxy** (via gRPC/HTTP) for every action; the proxy returns an allow/deny plus audit metadata. |
|12| **RBAC v2 Launch Checklist (Enterprise Controls)** | Confluence – *rbac‑v2‑launch‑checklist* | Lists the Console‑specific validation steps (role‑template import, permission‑search sanity‑check, audit‑log export enablement) that must pass before GA. |
|13| **Customer‑Security‑Questionnaire Answer Draft – RBAC v2** | Confluence – *customer‑security‑questionnaire‑answer‑draft‑rbac‑v2* | Shows the external‑facing wording that is derived from the Console’s RBAC behaviour (what a customer will see when they query permissions or audit logs). |
|14| **Break‑Glass Access (RBAC v2) Procedure** | Confluence – *break‑glass‑access‑rbac‑v2* | Defines a temporary elevation path that the Console must query (‑‑ “request emergency admin?”) and record in the audit trail. |
|15| **RBAC v2 Enterprise Controls PRD** | Confluence – *prd‑rbac‑v2‑enterprise‑controls* | The product‑level source that drives the Console’s RBAC feature set (role creation, delegation, audit‑log export). |

**Why these records matter**

- **RBAC direction** – Items 1‑5, 8, 12, 15 give the **policy model**, **permission catalog**, and **risk/launch gating** that make it clear the platform now enforces *least‑privilege, deny‑by‑default* across all surfaces.
- **Console‑query direction** – Items 6, 9, 10, 11, 13, 14 spell out **how the Console must query** the policy engine, what UI elements expose the results, and how audit evidence is attached to every query (including break‑glass and audit‑trail views).

Together these source records constitute the **complete, traceable evidence** for the current RBAC implementation and the Console‑side query behavior that customers and auditors rely on.

Source event id: activity-23457-1b81e7e8e563
Source app: github
Source URL: /github?repo=redwood&tab=pulls&pr=23457
Actor: sofia-ivanova
Occurred at: 1741262400000
Content:
[LTS Backport] Fix scheduler starvation edge case under bursty traffic + add regression reproducer

Context
We hit an edge case during the LTS pilot where a small fraction of requests could sit in the scheduler queue for an unbounded amount of time under bursty traffic. This manifested as occasional multi-minute tail latency spikes even though the system was otherwise healthy (no crash, no OOM), and it was hard to catch in steady-state load tests.

Root cause (high level)
In the request admission loop, when a request fails admission due to transient capacity constraints (e.g., batch token budget / KV budget), we requeue it and retry soon. Under a specific burst pattern (short prompts arriving continuously while a few long-prefill requests repeatedly fail admission), the fairness cursor/deficit bookkeeping could keep selecting the same requeued long request near the head of its queue. Because we did not treat repeated admission failures as “yielding” events, the scheduler could effec...

---

Source event id: activity-642-4662a4841569
Source app: github
Source URL: /github?repo=redwood-sdk-python&tab=pulls&pr=642
Actor: brian-murphy
Occurred at: 1741262400000
Content:
sdk: raise ResidencyPolicyViolationError for data residency enforcement blocks

Context: Hosted data residency enforcement returns a deterministic error when a request would violate the org/project/API-key region policy (see ADR-022 + gateway work in ENG-4833). Today the Python SDK surfaces this as a generic APIError, which makes it hard for customers to catch and handle (e.g. prompt the admin to update allowed regions) and for support to quickly pull request traces.

What this PR does: Adds a dedicated exception type, ResidencyPolicyViolationError, and maps residency enforcement responses into it. The exception exposes (a) error_code (machine readable), (b) request_id (for support + audit correlation), and (c) help_url (docs link for remediation). The rest of the response payload is still preserved on the base error for debugging.

Behavior: - If the API returns HTTP 409 with error.code matching residency enforcement (e.g. RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION), raise Resi...

---

Actor: kenji-nakamura
Occurred at: 1742558400000
Content:
Console audit trail view for Dedicated rollout actions (promote/abort/override/hold)

Goal
Design an audit trail view in Redwood Console for Dedicated staged rollout actions (create/update/promote/abort/hold/force actions) with filters, export UX, and RBAC-aware (admin-only) fields.

Context
Dedicated staged rollouts introduce explicit stage promotions with gates and override escape hatches (force-promote, hold, force-rollback). Regulated and enterprise customers (and our internal SRE/oncall) need an auditable record of:
- who did what, when, and why
- what changed (stage, plan, policy)
- whether it was an override
- what evidence/gates were evaluated at the time
- how this ties back to change management processes and incident review

Non-goals (v1)
- Full SIEM configuration workflow in Console (we’ll support export/download and rely on existing audit export pipelines where available)
- Arbitrary custom audit event types outside staged rollouts

Primary personas
1) Cus...

Source event id: activity-des-322-342f45ccde25
